Thank you for the detailed issue. I recreated it and fixed it. Version 0.7.4.0 has the fix. Please submit any other issues you encounter so I can make the tool better for everyone. By the way, in v0.7.4.0 you can now click variables in the "Locals" section to view their fields, instead of manually creating Expressions.

In case you are interested in the source of the problem, there were two issues. The first was that gdbgui was not wrapping variable expressions in quotes, so gdb attempted to evaluate an invalid expression (which was the error you were getting).

Even after that was fixed, the information returned by gdb was pretty useless. It described some internal methods to c++ vectors. To make this output more useful, I added the ` -enable-pretty-printing` option by default, which gdb has had since gdb version 7.0. It can be changed in the Settings section (gear on top right of gdbgui).

yes, i use it with my 1602 lcd with i2c backpack. Here the driver(?) i've used:
```python
# -*- coding: utf-8 -*-
# Original code found at:
# https://gist.github.com/DenisFromHR/cc863375a6e19dce359d

"""
Compiled, mashed and generally mutilated 2014-2015 by Denis Pleic
Made available under GNU GENERAL PUBLIC LICENSE

# Modified Python I2C library for Raspberry Pi
# as found on http://www.recantha.co.uk/blog/?p=4849
# Joined existing 'i2c_lib.py' and 'lcddriver.py' into a single library
# added bits and pieces from various sources
# By DenisFromHR (Denis Pleic)
# 2015-02-10, ver 0.1

"""

import smbus
from time import sleep

# i2c bus (0 -- original Pi, 1 -- Rev 2 Pi)
I2CBUS = 2

# LCD Address
ADDRESS = 0x27

class i2c_device:
	def __init__(self, addr, port=I2CBUS):
		self.addr = addr
		self.bus = smbus.SMBus(port)

# Write a single command
	def write_cmd(self, cmd):
		self.bus.write_byte(self.addr, cmd)
		sleep(0.0001)

# Write a command and argument
	def write_cmd_arg(self, cmd, data):
		self.bus.write_byte_data(self.addr, cmd, data)
		sleep(0.0001)

# Write a block of data
	def write_block_data(self, cmd, data):
		self.bus.write_block_data(self.addr, cmd, data)
		sleep(0.0001)

# Read a single byte
	def read(self):
		return self.bus.read_byte(self.addr)

# Read
	def read_data(self, cmd):
		return self.bus.read_byte_data(self.addr, cmd)

# Read a block of data
	def read_block_data(self, cmd):
		return self.bus.read_block_data(self.addr, cmd)


# commands
LCD_CLEARDISPLAY = 0x01
LCD_RETURNHOME = 0x02
LCD_ENTRYMODESET = 0x04
LCD_DISPLAYCONTROL = 0x08
LCD_CURSORSHIFT = 0x10
LCD_FUNCTIONSET = 0x20
LCD_SETCGRAMADDR = 0x40
LCD_SETDDRAMADDR = 0x80

# flags for display entry mode
LCD_ENTRYRIGHT = 0x00
LCD_ENTRYLEFT = 0x02
LCD_ENTRYSHIFTINCREMENT = 0x01
LCD_ENTRYSHIFTDECREMENT = 0x00

# flags for display on/off control
LCD_DISPLAYON = 0x04
LCD_DISPLAYOFF = 0x00
LCD_CURSORON = 0x02
LCD_CURSOROFF = 0x00
LCD_BLINKON = 0x01
LCD_BLINKOFF = 0x00

# flags for display/cursor shift
LCD_DISPLAYMOVE = 0x08
LCD_CURSORMOVE = 0x00
LCD_MOVERIGHT = 0x04
LCD_MOVELEFT = 0x00

# flags for function set
LCD_8BITMODE = 0x10
LCD_4BITMODE = 0x00
LCD_2LINE = 0x08
LCD_1LINE = 0x00
LCD_5x10DOTS = 0x04
LCD_5x8DOTS = 0x00

# flags for backlight control
LCD_BACKLIGHT = 0x08
LCD_NOBACKLIGHT = 0x00

Rs = 0b00000001 # Register select bit
En = 0b00000100 # Enable bit
Rw = 0b00000010 # Read/Write bit


class lcd:
	#initializes objects and lcd
	def __init__(self):
		self.lcd_device = i2c_device(ADDRESS)

		self.lcd_write(0x03)
		self.lcd_write(0x03)
		self.lcd_write(0x03)
		self.lcd_write(0x02)

		self.lcd_write(LCD_FUNCTIONSET | LCD_2LINE | LCD_5x8DOTS | LCD_4BITMODE)
		self.lcd_write(LCD_DISPLAYCONTROL | LCD_DISPLAYON)
		self.lcd_write(LCD_CLEARDISPLAY)
		self.lcd_write(LCD_ENTRYMODESET | LCD_ENTRYLEFT)
		sleep(0.2)


	# clocks EN to latch command
	def lcd_strobe(self, data):
		self.lcd_device.write_cmd(data | En | LCD_BACKLIGHT)
		sleep(.0005)
		self.lcd_device.write_cmd(((data & ~En) | LCD_BACKLIGHT))
		sleep(.0001)

	def lcd_write_four_bits(self, data):
		self.lcd_device.write_cmd(data | LCD_BACKLIGHT)
		self.lcd_strobe(data)

	# write a command to lcd
	def lcd_write(self, cmd, mode=0):
		self.lcd_write_four_bits(mode | (cmd & 0xF0))
		self.lcd_write_four_bits(mode | ((cmd << 4) & 0xF0))

	# write a character to lcd (or character rom) 0x09: backlight | RS=DR<
	# works!
	def lcd_write_char(self, charvalue, mode=1):
		self.lcd_write_four_bits(mode | (charvalue & 0xF0))
		self.lcd_write_four_bits(mode | ((charvalue << 4) & 0xF0))

	# put string function with optional char positioning
	def lcd_display_string(self, string, line=1, pos=0):
	 if line == 1:
		pos_new = pos
	 elif line == 2:
		pos_new = 0x40 + pos
	 elif line == 3:
		pos_new = 0x14 + pos
	 elif line == 4:
		pos_new = 0x54 + pos

	 self.lcd_write(0x80 + pos_new)

	 for char in string:
		self.lcd_write(ord(char), Rs)

	# clear lcd and set to home
	def lcd_clear(self):
		self.lcd_write(LCD_CLEARDISPLAY)
		self.lcd_write(LCD_RETURNHOME)

	# define backlight on/off (lcd.backlight(1); off= lcd.backlight(0)
	def backlight(self, state): # for state, 1 = on, 0 = off
		if state == 1:
			self.lcd_device.write_cmd(LCD_BACKLIGHT)
		elif state == 0:
			self.lcd_device.write_cmd(LCD_NOBACKLIGHT)

	# add custom characters (0 - 7)
	def lcd_load_custom_chars(self, fontdata):
		self.lcd_write(0x40);
		for char in fontdata:
			for line in char:
				self.lcd_write_char(line)
```

In the current state of things, yes, the separation is not very obvious. The behaviour is controlled partly by variables inside `call` function, partly by `self.statistics` which works because under the hood it's a threadlocal storage. I'm suggesting a separation where `Retry` merely contains settings, or a combination of retry strategies of you're into design patterns. 

When you run `retry.call` multiple times, with threadlocal storage or not, the calls are separated because each invocation gets its own stack frame. But retry strategies make it problematic, because you cannot carry that stack frame around. I mean, technically, in world of Python you can, but that's not portable and looks bad. So `RetryCallState` plays a role of a stack frame that can be passed into the strategies (retry, wait) and callbacks. It contains all data related to a single invocation, just like a stack frame. And just like a function doesn't hold stack frames of its invocations, `Retry` doesn't quite hold `RetryCallState` instances, they rather live in the stack frames.

Reviewing some of the code, it seems that there is some kind of duplication between __del__() and _shutdown_resources().   In the big picture, I think that destroying a Kernel without some kind of controlled shutdown should be considered an error.

So, I've modified the _shutdown_resources() method so clean up pools in addition to its usual cleanup.  This should now happen automatically for the curio.run()  method or if you explicitly call Kernel.run(shutdown=True).   I've modified __del__() to raise an exception if it's invoked without some kind of cleanup taking place first (note: maybe a warning message is better?).   I've also made the Kernel class work as a context manager to give better control over the shutdown process.
I personally could live with that :) :+1:

I think my number one concern with maintaining a task tree in-kernel is simply the mechanics of maintaining it in a sane way.   It's very easy to create small, short-lived tasks in curio for all sorts of purposes.   I'd want to make sure that's reasonably efficient.   I'm also concerned about the whole garbage collection side of it.  For example do tasks stick around until they're explicitly joined?   Are there issues with "zombie" tasks.   That kind of thing.

Definitely want to think about how all of this would work with all of the corner cases.

One thing I'd like to flesh out more in curio is the monitor/debugging side of it.  The first release does have a debugging monitor, but I think there's more that could be done there.  I'm thinking along the lines of something like the Erlang etop functionality maybe.  That would be kind of cool.
Fixed by including the task id in the sleep queue to break ties in heapq.

The `type` REST API attribute is derived from the ZIgBee device type (per endpoint).  Unfortunately, there's multiple values for plugs, and not all devices use the correct value.  So far I've seen `"On/Off plug-in unit", `"Smart plug"`, and `"Dimmable plug-in unit"` (for the old Philips LivingWhites plug, which actually supports dimming, so you probably want to expose that as a light).

See https://github.com/dresden-elektronik/deconz-rest-plugin/blob/07fa1bf0fba5254b71667e59f25b6d5b0144ee68/light_node.cpp#L530 for a complete overview of the supported device types and corresponding values for `type`.

Basically homebridge-hue checks what `state` attributes are provided.  This used to work, until the innr SP 120 came along.  This smart plug actually supports a _Level Control_ cluster, so deCONZ creates a `state.bri` for it.  It doesn't actually support dimming, but it does react to the _Move Level with On/Off_ commands, I suspect because this is what the innr remote sends.  
I whitelisted the plug in homebridge-hue to ignore `state.bri` and expose it to HomeKit as an _Outlet_ instead of a _LightBulb_ service.

Note that there are also other devices that are exposed as a `/lights` resource, like sirens (`"Warning device"`), the Keen Smart vent (`"Level controllable output"`), and the Xiaomi curtain controller (`"Window covering device"`).

Okay, since this answer still pops up in the Google Search, I'll share my workaround for fixing this issue.

Main idea to install brew version of curl and force linking to get an up-to-date curl:

    $ curl --version
    curl 7.52.1 (x86_64-apple-darwin16.1.0) libcurl/7.52.1 OpenSSL/1.0.2j zlib/1.2.8 nghttp2/1.18.1

So you can later use pycurl, linked against your `libcurl` and `openssl`

    ```
brew install curl
brew link curl --force
brew install openssl
export LIBRARY_PATH=/usr/local/opt/openssl/lib
export CPATH=/usr/local/opt/openssl/include
pip --no-cache-dir install pycurl
python -c "import pycurl"
```

Hope that helps!

Hi,
there are 3 type of object class in ldap, abstract, structural and auxiliary. An entry can have only one structural object class and many auxiliary classes. The classes specify what attributes can be stored in the entry. The structural object class is defined at entry creation time and cannot be changed. Auxiliary object classes can be added at creation or later and can be removed. Usually you have to delete all the attributes related to the auxiliary class before removing it. Abstract classes are automatically added to the entry and follow the class hierarchy. They cannot be manually removed.

So you can add or remove only auxiliary classes using the standard modify() operation.

One possible strategy (for the `2 x 2` case) would be to change the system as we go when a multiple root is detected. (This assumes `DF != [[0, 0], [0, 0]]` at the root. If that occurs, then this approach will fail.)

Write

```
F = [f(s, t)] ==> DF = [grad(f)]
    [g(s, t)]          [grad(g)]
```

Assuming `DF` is rank one, then we can assume at the root

```
p grad(f) + q grad(g) = [0, 0]
```

for some `p, q`. By checking the signs of `grad(f)` and `grad(g)` (even if we consider `0` to be a 3rd "sign"), we can guarantee that one of `grad(f) + grad(g)` or `grad(f) - grad(g)` will not be a zero row. Thus, we can replace `f, g` with `f +/- g, grad(f) x grad(g)` (here `grad(f) x grad(g) = det(DF)`, which we have detected to be zero at the root).

For the case above, we perform this process:

```
f0 = 2(4s^2 - 8t^2 + 1)
g0 = (2s - 4t + 1)(2s + 4t - 3)
DF0 = [8, -16]
      [0,  0 ]
```

so then we use

```
f1 = f0 + g0 = 12 s^2 - 32 t^2 - 4s + 16 t - 1
g1 = grad(f0) x grad(g0) = -128(2st - 2s + t)
DF1 = [  8, -16 ]
      [128, -256]
```

and then

```
f2 = f1 + g1 = 12 s^2 - 256 st - 32 t^2 + 252s - 112t - 1
g2 = grad(f1) x grad(g1) = -512(12s^2 + 32t^2 + 4s - 40t + 7)
DF2 = [  136, -272]
      [-8192, 4096]
```

Thanks for the feedback.

- Yes, your 2nd comment seems about the same error as in #228 , so yes, the error is not fatal, you can ignore it, just that there won't be any caching for hash calculation during upload/download.
 
- The `UnicodeDecodeError` is a bit strange, as I've never heard people having this problem loading/saving hash cache. Maybe your system encoding is not `UTF-8`? I suspect it's related to system locale configurations / garbled file names.

- You can inspect `~/.bypy/bypy.hashcache.json` to see why it failed to load/save, and you can safely delete it to get rid of the problem. However, if the problem re-occurs after you run `bypy` again, then I think you need to check system locale/file names for root cause.

- To make sure cache saving/loading errors won't affect normal operations, I've updated the package, please run `pip install -U bypy` to update to the latest version. The latest version (1.6.1) won't abort due to cache loading/saving errors.

This can probably be achieved right out of the box if you'd have different `usmUser` for snmpsim (e.g. for SNMP commands) and notification module (e.g. for traps).

The other solution that comes to my mind is to default the authentication and privacy protocols in notification module to the same ones as being configured for this `usmUser` by snmpsim.

Finally, may be we could make notification module instantiating its own `snmpEngine` and that way having independent `usmUser` configuration. This approach looks marginally logical to me as snmpsim, being a SNMP agent, is supposed to run the only snmpEngine so that SNMP commands and notifications would come from the same snmpEngine / usmUser...

I did try the homebrew tap but it failed with the same error (I don't really want to try it again because I currently have a working setup :).

Building again from a fresh checkout causes the same problem - but I have just noticed this:
```
$ python ./setup.py build
include header path: /usr/local/include/GraphicsMagick/
boost lib: boost_python-mt
library path: /usr/local/lib/libGraphicsMagick++
GraphicsMagick version: 1.3.20
...
```
`setup.py` sees the graphicsmagick version as 1.3.20, but I'm fairly sure 1.3.23 is installed:
```
$ GraphicsMagick-config --version
1.3.23
$ GraphicsMagick++-config --version
1.3.23
$ pkg-config --modversion GraphicsMagick++
1.3.23
$ which GraphicsMagick-config
/usr/local/bin/GraphicsMagick-config
```

Further digging shows that it's getting the version from the `.pc` file in an older version of graphicsmagick which is at `/usr/local/Cellar/graphicsmagick/1.3.20`. The build process then tries to build against the currently-installed version, which is 1.3.23.

This is because of `/usr/local/Cellar/graphicsmagick` being added to `search_include_dirs` in setup.py. If there's more than one version of graphicsmagick installed it'll probably return the oldest version first, which seems wrong.

Not sure why that code is scanning the include dirs for `.pc` files, or whether it's ever right to use a homebrew-installed version of graphicsmagick which isn't linked (homebrew will link the `.pc` files to `/usr/local/lib/pkgconfig/`).

I can work around this by changing [line 128](https://github.com/hhatto/pgmagick/blob/master/setup.py#L128) of setup.py to search `search_pkgconfig_dirs` before `search_include_dirs`:

```
_version = get_version_from_pc(search_pkgconfig_dirs + search_include_dirs, GMCPP_PC)
```

After this change it builds successfully.

Can you show me what you are doing in your `idle terminal` (i.e. what did you do to generate the above traceback)?

> you need to have a thread dedicated to this and some sort of an ID

This is not possible with the python logging framework. You cannot handle the logging in a separate thread. Also, if you change a logging handler to write to some other file, it changes for all threads because there's only 1 logger for netmiko with and attached handler.

> Can't logging handle this (assuming you handle the concurrency issue?

No, it cannot because of the above.

> You can you show me what you want versus what you are getting?

Have a look at the 2 gists in my second reply.

> Can you explain what you mean by a "structured way"

With structured, I mean that every session netmiko creates with a device (optionally) results in a separate file with only the communication with that device. You can name these files in some way so you can find them back easily and see what happened during the communication with the device.

For example, imagine running netmiko as part of Celery tasks. Every task does some kind of change on a device. Like configuring an interface based on user input on 3000 devices.

Now, imagine that 3% of those tasks fail because netmiko somehow didn't properly understand the response of the device. (slight difference in response between software version, latency related issues, ...) You could then go and look in the file for that specific task and see what the device responded and where it went wrong.

This is nearly impossible to do when you cannot split the conversation with a device in a separate file.

> It seems pretty unlikely you would log to the syslog server

Well, in case you run netmiko inside of Django and your Django code already emits log message to a syslog server, you could end up with syslogging all debug messages of netmiko.
There's also another part to this story. Because netmiko sends those logs out as DEBUG messages, you're forced to also set the overall logging level to DEBUG. Every package apart from Django and Netmiko that you use and that emits debug logs will all of a sudden also become visible.

Besides all that, the context I'm talking about is "not" for debugging netmiko. It's for keeping a record of communications with devices. So the focus is different.

> In principle one can use the non-overlapping viz for chords too, right?

Yup, and you can use overlapping for segments, which is perhaps better depending on what you're trying to communicate.  For example, if you have small segments `A->B->C`, this is hard to see as a larger pattern in the disjoint plot, but the shapes pop visually in the overlapping plot.  The disjoint plot is better for overlaying on a spectrogram though, so YMMV.



> The issue is that given the large chord vocabulary, mapping each chord to a color and providing a legend can also get unwieldy/unhelpful.

That's one issue. Another issue is that we quickly run out of color/texture-based styles in large vocabularies, so if you're relying solely on rectangle style (and not vertical position) to differentiate, it's easy to get lost.  We can chain up property cyclers to move from flat to cross-hatched, then to alpha channels, etc, but the returns on this diminish quickly.

The bigger issue is that chord intervals are often too small to fit a meaningful text decoration inside.  If we have `%matplotlib nbagg` or mpld3 enabled, we could use hover text for this, but I don't think it should be the primary mode of communicating segment identity.

Finally, we probably should put some thought into having some way to impose consistent styling of labels across plots.  When comparing an estimate to a reference (say, for chords), you probably want the same labels in the same positions with the same styles. I think the simplest way to do this is to allow the user to supply an ordered list `reference_labels` that are used to populate the first `n` positions.  Any labels in the input that are not in `reference_labels` are then printed starting at positions`n+1`.  This way, you could plot a reference and estimate side-by-side, or even on top of each other (with alpha channels and/or hatching), and still have a sane result.

Two options come to mind:

* Use python's [logging](https://docs.python.org/2/library/logging.html) instead of `sys.stderr` and add a `--log-level` parameter to the argparse. Open to pull requests on doing this.

* Output stderr to a temporary file, then print it only if the exit code is not 0.

```
python acme_tiny.py --account-key ./account.key \
  --csr ./domain.csr --acme-dir /var/www/challenges/ \
  > signed.crt 2> /tmp/errors.log \
|| cat /tmp/errors.log 1>&2
```
Append the root certificate also, which you can find here: https://letsencrypt.org/certs/isrgrootx1.pem

I think this is a dangerous pattern to introduce. It can encourage all sorts of manual failover pathologies...for example the "failed" endpoint A has come back and now the "healthy" endpoint B is actually down, so you're preemptively sending your request to a failed endpoint.  

The best approach is to try the publish and retry it after timeout. If your app has some sort of SLA that would benefit from a centralized view of endpoints being up/down, that really should be in located in your application logic where you can imbue the specific intelligence you need for your use case. I don't think this generalizes well.

According to [this doc page](https://mottie.github.io/tablesorter/docs/example-options-headers-parser.html) & my recollection of reviewing the code earlier, the parser value is indeed to save on having the nature of the data be auto-detected, and then the values that are generated by this choice of parser are naturally independant of how they are then sorted by the chosen sorter.  
To use "sorter" to also determine the "parser" seems to overly conflate the purposes of the two.

Obviously you know how it was intended to be, yet this key name change fixed my problem (how else to efficiently define a cell's data as coming from an arbitrary attribute/data extension of the parent row, at the parsing & caching stage?), is consistent with the actual names of the parse->sort stages, and didn't break anything that I know of so far.  
It's my impression that were I to only define a sorter per header, that there'd still be a slower process of auto-detecting the text/numeric nature of the extracted & cached data values. And then the potential of mismatching between this nature/type and any defined sorter/parser (e.g. seemingly textual data being fed to a parser that's defined as `type: 'numeric'`.

I have several different use-cases where the value to sort a given column by is found via lookup into another data structure, and it seemed by design that the parser stage would be supplied with the custom function(s) to do this so that the values are cached, rather than regenerated every time every cell is compared & sorted against every other (i.e. the lookup performed by the sorting definition).

I wasn't thinking of changing the `params` (in Python) after the `.prepare`. If pyodbc can take care of optimizing the parameters based on the information it gets back from the server then that's great. 

It's more about having control over when a prepare takes place, sort of a continuation of my comment [here](https://github.com/mkleehammer/pyodbc/issues/214#issuecomment-286589204). So not only could an explicit `.prepare` be used to avoid sending the command text over and over (via sp_prepexec, as it does now), it could also let pyodbc get the information it needs to manage the parameters more effectively.

According to [the ODBC driver documentation](https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/sql/relational-databases/native-client-odbc-date-time/data-type-support-for-odbc-date-and-time-improvements) (you may have to turn off stylesheets if you do not see all the columns in the table) `SQL_TIMESTAMP` is for ODBC 2.0 while 3.0 uses `SQL_TYPE_TIMESTAMP`.

`datetime` and `datetime2` both map to SQL type `SQL_TYPE_TIMESTAMP`.

from pptx import Presentation

class TableData:
    """

    def __init__(self, slide_title, gfk_qtype, series_names, cat_names, data, chart_type=XL_CHART_TYPE.COLUMN_CLUSTERED, series_overlap = 0):

I liked and used the code @sclem posted, but I ran into the issue that when the image placeholder had some animations on them, it would lose these properties.
I adapted his code and used the "crop_bottom" and equivalent methods _(which seems to be recommended in the documentation)_ to create the following method. I'm just sharing the code in case other people run into this problem as well. I suspect the code can be optimised further, but this works for me:

```
def _add_image(slide, placeholder_id, image_url):
    placeholder = slide.placeholders[placeholder_id]

    # Calculate the image size of the image
    im = Image.open(image_url)
    width, height = im.size

    # Calculate and set max width/height for target size, such that the placeholder doesn't "zoom in" the image
    ratio = min(placeholder.width / float(width), placeholder.height / float(height))
    placeholder.height = int(height * ratio)
    placeholder.width = int(width * ratio)

    # Insert the picture
    placeholder = placeholder.insert_picture(image_url)

    # Calculate ratios to compare them
    image_ratio = width / height
    placeholder_ratio = placeholder.width / placeholder.height

    # Placeholder width too wide:
    if image_ratio < placeholder_ratio:
        ratio_difference = placeholder_ratio - image_ratio
        difference_on_each_side = ratio_difference / 2  # Because we want the image centered on the placeholder
        placeholder.crop_left = -difference_on_each_side
        placeholder.crop_right = -difference_on_each_side
    # Placeholder height to high
    else:
        ratio_difference = image_ratio - placeholder_ratio
        difference_on_each_side = ratio_difference / 2
        placeholder.crop_bottom = -difference_on_each_side
        placeholder.crop_top = -difference_on_each_side
```

Here's an example which covers the above test:

```Python
import midi

def time_diff_to_tick(time, resolution, tempo):
    return int(time/(60./float(tempo)/float(resolution)))

# Instantiate a MIDI Pattern (contains a list of tracks)
pattern = midi.Pattern()
# Instantiate a MIDI Track (contains a list of MIDI events)
track = midi.Track()
# Append the track to the pattern
pattern.append(track)

# Tempo 120 at 0s
tempo = midi.SetTempoEvent(tick=0)
tempo.set_bpm(120)
track.append(tempo)

# Time signature 4/4 at 0s
time = midi.TimeSignatureEvent(tick=0)
time.set_numerator(4)
time.set_denominator(4)
track.append(time)

# Tempo change at .8s to 100 qpm
tempo = midi.SetTempoEvent(
    tick=time_diff_to_tick(.8, pattern.resolution, 120))
tempo.set_bpm(100)
track.append(tempo)

# Time signature change at 1.7s to 6/8
time = midi.TimeSignatureEvent(
    tick=time_diff_to_tick(.9, pattern.resolution, 100))
time.set_numerator(6)
time.set_denominator(8)
track.append(time)

# Time signature change at 2.8s to 4/4
time = midi.TimeSignatureEvent(
    tick=time_diff_to_tick(1.1, pattern.resolution, 100))
time.set_numerator(4)
time.set_denominator(4)
track.append(time)

# Add a long note event to force pretty_midi to report lots of beats
on = midi.NoteOnEvent(tick=100, velocity=20, pitch=midi.G_3)
track.append(on)
off = midi.NoteOffEvent(tick=1000, pitch=midi.G_3)
track.append(off)

# Save the MIDI file
midi.write_midifile("example.mid", pattern)

pm = pretty_midi.PrettyMIDI('example.mid')
print pm.get_tempo_changes()
print pm.time_signature_changes
print pm.get_beats()
```

Certainly sounds like a metadata problem-- it's 2/3 compatible and tested from 2.6 through 3.4 (and should work on 3.5, though I'm going to add another line to the the travis file to explicitly test that).

It has been impossible to reproduce the scenario from above in which `pyfolio` believes that some trades are short in a long-only strategy.

But putting it to work with intraday data (30 min too) has shown that the sub-day implementation had several points with room for improvement.

The naive assumption from above, *daily data* was based on the fact that pyfolio seems to offer *daily* as the lowest level of resolution for the analysis output and anything below that is *resampled*. The latter not having been taken into account.

The major area of incompatibility here, due to the naive assumption, is that the output for the pyfolio specific analyzers considers the larger timeframe for data storage, without taking into account that it will be called several in the smaller timeframe, effectively losing most of the output and in some cases all.

In the case of *transactions*, the storage was being overwritten for each 30 minute bar and unless the last 30 minute bar of the day happened to be one with a transaction, then the amount of transactions for the given day would be empty.

In the test case (3 years of 3 minute data) the *Annual Return* provided by both *backtrader* and *pyfolio* (regardless of timeframe) has been a match and that's the puzzling thing with regards to the scenario depicted above.

In any case the changes to properly support the smaller timeframes giving *pyfiolio* the right will be polished along the day, re-tested and pushed, which I hope will also cure the problems from above.

Thanks @fciaccia, You are right, I was parsing the reverse field the other way around.

My recollection is that pack and alignment are different attributes return by clang.
In ctypeslib, __pack__ will always be True to ensure that python ctypes fields are actually in the correct index.
If the alignment provided by tells clang says a field is not packed next the the previous one, a Padding field will be inserted in-between.

Given that ctypeslib is aware of multi-architecture issues, it's a requirement.
You just have to trust that the code & alignment, issued for the platform chosen by clang is correct.

You cannot ask a python 2.7 ctypes lib on a x86 arch to know how to pack a structure for a arm 128 arch.

OK, let's think about it. Arrow provides several formatting methods:

- strftime (the one you currently are calling)
- format (the one that supports ISO and other formats)
- humanize (this one permits things like "2 hours ago", etc)

So, the question is how can your package support invoking alternative formatters?. By default -- for backwards compatibility -- you want to call strftime, but allow developers to pick another formatter like 'format' or 'humanize' of Arrow. I think we should be liberal in what we allow. Who knows what reason someone might have for wanting this behavior? Maybe they just want to change from:
```
@dataclass
class Base:
   x: int
   # other useful fields or methods
```
to:
```
@dataclass
class C(Base):
   x: int = field(hash=False)
```
That is, the same as Base, but `x` would no longer be used in hashes of `C` instances.

I think if mypy complains, then that would make most people not use the feature, but I wouldn't want to limit someone who really wants it.

I guess it could be a member of the class, if it had a default value.

Let's first look at how `ClassVar` works, as an example:

```
>>> @dataclass
... class C:
...   i: int
...   cls_var: ClassVar[List[int]]
...
>>> c=C(0)
>>> c.i
0
>>> list(fields(c).keys())
['i']
>>> C.cls_var
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<stdin>", line 1, in <module>
AttributeError: type object 'C' has no attribute 'cls_var'
```

There's never any assignment to C.x, so there's no attribute defined on the class. And, it's not a field, so there's no per-instance attribute. Maybe you should be required to have a default value, but I say leave it up to the class author.

Here's how `ClassVar` works with a default value:
```
>>> @dataclass
... class D:
...   i: int
...   cls_var: ClassVar[List[int]] = []
...
>>> d=D(0)
>>> d.cls_var
[]
>>> d.__dict__
{'i': 0}
>>> D.cls_var
[]
```

So, there is now a class attribute `cls_var` because the default value assigned to it. Note that the above behavior with respect to `cls_var`. where it only exists if it has a default value, is the same for a non-dataclass class.

I would propose that an `InitVar` field would work exactly the same way, as far as how it's handled as a field and as a class attribute. If you gave it a default value, it would be a class attribute, but I don't think anyone would ever look for that class attribute.

Then the only difference between a `ClassVar` and an `InitVar` is that `InitVar`'s are passed to `__dataclass_post_init__()`.

One thing that might be worth trying as a workaround is to unset `Accept-Encoding`. You will have to download [jenkins.py](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/gvalkov/jenkins-webapi/master/jenkins.py) and apply this patch:

```diff
diff --git i/jenkins.py w/jenkins.py
index fb62af3..18cf1f0 100644
--- i/jenkins.py
+++ w/jenkins.py
@@ -452,6 +452,7 @@ class Server(object):
             'auth': self.auth,
             'cert': cert,
             'verify': verify,
+            'headers': {'Accept-Encoding': None},
         }
 
     def __repr__(self):
```

You can then run `PYTHONPATH=/path/to/dir/containing/jenkins.py jenkins-makejobs-svn -t config.yaml`.

As far as I recall, RabbitMQ doesn't allow you to bind a queue to the default exchange. The default exchange will always send the message to the queue named by the routing key. So you shouldn't really be attempting to bind your queue to the default exchange in the first place.

The debate here, therefore, is whether `asynqp` should allow you to bind a queue to the default exchange (but silently do nothing) or throw an exception. A quick survey of other clients shows that [Pika doesn't appear to handle it at all](https://github.com/pika/pika/blob/master/pika/channel.py#L752-L776) and [Kombu silently does nothing](https://github.com/celery/kombu/blob/5a76eddd2b0ee4670e565088c556c06c0ff740ab/kombu/abstract.py#L77-L83).

I have a slight preference for throwing an exception when you bind to the default exchange. Better to make people think about what they're doing. (The existing error message could certainly be improved upon, though.)

There is no way to use `shorten()` without collapsing whitespace. The [textwrap code](https://github.com/python/cpython/blob/188aedf8bb623d41302e10503268b0852ea91134/Lib/textwrap.py#L405-L405) does a `text.strip().split()` which will inherently collapse all whitespace, regardless of the `replace_whitespace` or `drop_whitespace` options.  It's arguable it should not work that way—but that's the way it currently works. `ansiwrap` just follows core `textwrap` on that decision. 

Would it be possible to create a non-whitespace-replacing version of shorten? Sure. But it's not trivial, because the core `TextWrapper` object, while it has a `max_lines` parameter, has no `max_characters` that it implicitly obeys. 

If you want just a string truncate that respects whitespace, you'd have to do that at a pretty low level. For example, a bare-bones function (without a fancy placeholder) might look something like this:

```python
import re
from ansiwrap.core import ansilen, ansi_terminate_lines
from ansiwrap.ansistate import ANSIState

FULLANSIRE = re.compile('(\x1b\\[(?:K|.*?m))')
    
def truncate(text, width):
    """
    Return the first width characters of text.
    Width is computed counting only non-ANSI
    sequences.
    """
    if ansilen(text) <= width:
        return text
    parts = FULLANSIRE.split(text)
    total = 0
    accum = []
    for part in parts:
        p_len = ansilen(part)
        if total + p_len <= width:
            # more room, add more
            total += p_len
            accum.append(part)
        else:
            # no more room - possibly truncate last part
            if FULLANSIRE.match(part):
                break
            else:
                remaining = width - total
                partial = part[:remaining]
                accum.append(partial)
                break
    # combine to feed through line-oriented terminator function
    combined = ''.join(accum)
    term = ansi_terminate_lines([combined])
    truncated = '\n'.join(term)
    return truncated
```

If you really wanted to flesh out this idea, it'd probably be best to add a `placeholder` option. And of course you'd need tests.

My implementation is like below :  converting txt input and translate Chinese character 
 to English one
```
# -*- coding: utf-8 -*-
import os, shutil
import unicodedata
from pypinyin import pinyin, lazy_pinyin, Style

table = {ord(f):ord(t) for f,t in zip(
     u'，。！？、【】（）％＃＠＆１２３４５６７８９０',
     u',.!?,[]()%#@&1234567890')}

def CHS2pinyin(CHS_text):
    pinyin_text = pinyin(CHS_text, style=Style.TONE3)
    list = [x for j in pinyin_text for x in j]
    output_ = ' '.join(str(v) for v in list)
    t = unicodedata.normalize('NFKC', output_)
    return t.translate(table)

input_dir = './'
input_file = 'text_output.txt'
CHS_FILE = 'text_input.txt'

with open(os.path.join(input_dir, input_file), 'w') as file:
    with open(os.path.join(input_dir, CHS_FILE), encoding='utf-8') as f:
        for line in f:
            output = CHS2pinyin(line)
            file.write('{}'.format(output))

if os.path.isfile(input_file):
    print("File generated success, please go next step!")
```

I believe the same thing is happening for ImmutableListMultiMap (maybe just the version from Guava.)  The serializer seems to write the number of key/value sets as block data, then for each set, writes:  1.) the key, 2.) blockdata with the count of the values, 3.) the list of values.  In most cases, this seems to get sucked into the annotations and at least the stream is consistent (though the keys/values are not accessible in any convenient way.)  But for an EmptyImmuntableListMultimap, the block data saying there are 0 k/v sets becomes the value for the following object.

I think one thing that might help is checking not just the classdesc flags, but the collapsed flags for all superclasses, for SC_WRITE_METHOD.  It seems any time a parent class uses SC_WRITE_METHOD, there's a chance (nay, a guarantee) that one part of the object has used a blockdata--though it may be in the middle of a three-class hierarchy!  The current code doesn't seem to OR the flags together, but instead uses the values from the lowest level class.  It's also not clear how to interpret this when a class is referenced and that class has SC_WRITE_METHOD, or how to interpret it in the context of an object (where the classdec has already been read--it seems that it's being used for object data, not one-time class description data.)

pip, easy_install and buildout suffer from same problems:

- if github is down, installations will not work (pypi provides mirroring system of more than 10 servers)
- for each package a lot of request/responses are made, and if you provide tarball on pypi, installers don't have to crawl additional packages through download links

All it takes to fix that is: 

python setup.py egg_info -RDb "" sdist register upload

or use jarn.mkrelease: 

mkrelease -d pypi

1. Are you on a wired connection or is the Homegear machine using Wifi? There once was another user who had issues when using wifi, and switching to wired resolved the issue.
2. What's interesting are the last few lines of home-assistant.log:

```
17-05-02 00:09:36 DEBUG (Thread-13) [pyhomematic._hm] RPCFunctions.event: interface_id = homeassistant-wireless, address = NEQXXXXXXX:4, value_key = VALVE_STATE, value = 0
17-05-02 00:09:36 INFO (Thread-13) [pyhomematic.devicetypes.generic] HMGeneric.event: address=NEQXXXXXXX:4, interface_id=homeassistant-wireless, key=VALVE_STATE, value=0
17-05-02 00:09:36 DEBUG (Thread-13) [pyhomematic.devicetypes.generic] HMDevice.event: Using callback <bound method HMDevice._hm_event_callback of <Entity BZ_Thermostat: auto>> 
17-05-02 00:09:36 DEBUG (Thread-13) [homeassistant.components.homematic] BZ_Thermostat received event 'VALVE_STATE' value: 0
17-05-02 00:10:35 DEBUG (Thread-13) [pyhomematic._hm] RPCFunctions.event: interface_id = homeassistant-wireless, address = MEQXXXXXXX:0, value_key = RSSI_DEVICE, value = -67
17-05-02 00:10:40 INFO (Thread-13) [pyhomematic.devicetypes.generic] HMGeneric.event: address=MEQXXXXXXX:0, interface_id=homeassistant-wireless, key=RSSI_DEVICE, value=-67
17-05-02 00:11:07 DEBUG (Thread-13) [pyhomematic._hm] RPCFunctions.event: interface_id = homeassistant-wireless, address = MEQXXXXXXX:0, value_key = RSSI_DEVICE, value = -67
17-05-02 00:11:09 INFO (Thread-13) [pyhomematic.devicetypes.generic] HMGeneric.event: address=MEQXXXXXXX:0, interface_id=homeassistant-wireless, key=RSSI_DEVICE, value=-67
17-05-02 00:12:46 DEBUG (Thread-13) [pyhomematic._hm] RPCFunctions.event: interface_id = homeassistant-wireless, address = MEQXXXXXXX:0, value_key = RSSI_DEVICE, value = -67
17-05-02 00:12:59 INFO (Thread-13) [pyhomematic.devicetypes.generic] HMGeneric.event: address=MEQXXXXXXX:0, interface_id=homeassistant-wireless, key=RSSI_DEVICE, value=-67
```

Notice how before the last few RSSI_DEVICE events the other events always have at least two messages (1 debug, 1 info, sometimes more depending on device), which roughly happen at the same time. Throughout the logs those messages are never further apart than a second.
In the last few lines though it's 5, 2 and 13 seconds. To me this indicates, that maybe HASS is overloaded and struggeling to handle the events.
What I would suggest: add some sort of system monitor sensor to your HASS configuration and monitor the CPU load. Maybe there is something happening on your HASS machine that makes the HomeMatic thread stall, and because it takes too long to answer the event-message Homegear drops the connection. You're saying HASS is running within Docker on a NAS system. Have a look at the NAS logs too and see if maybe there is some sort of cron-job syncing whatever data. Or anything else. And most importantly: Have a look at the other non-HomeMatic components you are using in HASS. Maybe there's a buggy component that blocks all of HASS because of your ISP reconnect and nobody has discovered it yet.

Ah, okay, I think I'm understanding now. So we have two arrays: The volumetric data texture (VDT) and the transfer function (TF).

**The VDT:**
- has shape (N_x, N_y, N_z, 4), where N_* is the size of the data in the * direction.
- VDT[i,j,k,:] describes f(x_i, y_j, z_k) and the gradient at the same point.
- presumably contains 8 bit signed integers.

**The TF:**
- has shape (N,M,4) where N = M = 256 for 2D TF, or N = 256, M = 1 for 1D TF.
- TF[i,j,:] describes the RGBA values which will be assigned to a voxel with function value f_i and gradient value gradf_j, where f_0 = min(f), f_(N-1) = max(f), and gradf_0 = min(grad f), gradf_(M-1) = max(grad f)
- contains floating point values between 0 and 1.

Then, the RGBA values assigned to the voxel at point (x_i, y_j, z_k) are:
TF[ DTV[x_i, y_j, z_k, 0], DTV[x_i, y_j, z_k, 1], :]
(assuming that for a particular point, f is stored in column 0 and the magnitude of the gradient is stored in column 1, and the other two columns are the spherical angles)

The ``upload`` method on Redmine object just uploads a file to the Redmine and returns an assigned token that identifies this file inside Redmine. Then you can use this token to attach this uploaded file to the resource that supports attachments, for example Issue or Wikipage. If you just upload a file and don't attach it to the resource, it will just stay in Redmine, if you lose a token, you can't get it again through the REST API, you need to search for it manually inside the Redmine database.

That's all that you can do with it for now. And no, it's not possible to upload a file via ``upload`` and fetch it's url, BUT if you upload and then attach it to some Resource, it becomes an Attachment resource by itself (surprise, surprise), and then it will have an id, url and so on, and you can use ``redmine.attachment.get(ID)`` to get this info from it.

The low-level details of this process are described [here](http://www.redmine.org/projects/redmine/wiki/Rest_api#Attaching-files).

So are you looking for a sort of stateless authentication with flask security? Check out `login_manager.request_loader`.

Here's how you might implement this functionality using `flask-security`

```python
user_datastore = SQLAlchemyUserDatastore(db, User, Role)
security = Security(app, user_datastore)

@security.login_manager.request_loader
def load_user_from_request(req):
    api_key = req.args.get('api_key', req.headers.get('Authorization'))
    # retrieve and return a user object
```

Still experiencing the same issue on 0.4.2. My solution is incredibly hacky but connection cleanup works fine. (It's a monkey-patch)

```python
from ws4py.websocket import WebSocket as WebSocket_

class WebSocket(WebSocket_):	
        def _write(self, b):
		"""
		Trying to prevent a write operation
		on an already closed websocket stream.
		This cannot be bullet proof but hopefully
		will catch almost all use cases.
		"""
		if self.terminated or self.sock is None:
			raise RuntimeError("Cannot send on a terminated websocket")

		"""
		If the client disconnects in an unexpected way.
		"""
		try:
			self.sock.sendall(b)
		except BrokenPipeError:
			pass
```

Hi @nahidalam, First at all, please, tell me if something is not clear. English is not my mother tongue :-) (I'm French). Here is the use case for others messages queues (as Redis) : http://python-socketio.readthedocs.io/en/latest/#using-a-message-queue I illustrate the behavior in the attached picture : First we have two clients (client 1 and client 2) connected to the same instance of our server (a flask application by example). Theses clients can share messages because they are connected to the same Socket.io server. Then, due to performance issues, we add another instance of our application (horizontal scaling) and another client connects to the new instance. Because they are connected on two differents Socket.io servers, 'Client 1' and 'Client 2' can not share messages with 'Client 3'. Here appears the message queue. Each Socket.io server is connected to the message queue. Ex: 'Client 1' sends a message. The Socket.io server of the 'Instance 1' produces a message on a kafka topic. The Socket.io server of the 'Instance 2' has subscribed to the kafka topic, consumes the message and send it to the 'Client 3'. All this behaviour is transparent, all you need is configure your Socket.IO instance to use kafka (see below). Example of codes (requirement : python-kafka must be installed): ```python app = Flask(__name__) socketio = SocketIO(async_mode='eventlet') socketio.init_app(app, message_queue='kafka://localhost:9092') # Assume that Kafka is running on localhost (port 9092) ... # Just use socketio.on, send and emit as if you're not using message queue, this is transparent. ``` Of course, to use Kafka with Flask-SocketIO and python-socketio, you have to wait that my PR are integrated. (You could use my temporarly repos for testing, but i will not maintain them anymore, so please, consider to use miguel's repo for production). I hope I helped you. Hi @eayin2 

Your pull request has many errors like missing variables, all unit tests fail.

Some observations:

**In job definitions and apscheduler methods: job_id instead of id.**
We cannot rename this field, it will break compatibility with previous versions and APScheduler also expect a parameter **id** rather than **job_id**

**Invoking scheduler.start() within init_app(), because jobs are modified within init_app() (to update possible job definition changes to the existing jobstore jobs). Before modifying any jobs the scheduler has to be started.**
It seems you have jobs in your jobstore (possibly a DB job store) and you also have jobs in your config file, I don't think that is a good idea, you should keep all your jobs in only one place. Could you tell me why are you doing it?

Which causes babel to fail, because it assumes the source file to be
transpiled *will* be in a directory under the directory containing
node_modules.

It seems to me the proper solution is to not have the source files get
copied to ../public/static (that is happening when I do ./manage.py
collectstatic, because the source files are under /static/<app> )

Regarding the first case, Babel plugins are installed with npm and it has
> nothing to do with static_precompiler. The typical location of the
> plugins is the node_modules folder in your working directory.
>
> Regarding the second case, static_precompiler locates the source files
> (almost) the same way is Django does. It first looks for the files in your
> STATIC_ROOT folder,
>
and that is actually the problem. It is finding the copy of the source file
copied to STATIC_ROOT, and asking babel to transpile that version. But
STATIC_ROOT doesn't have node_modules, so babel can't find it plugins.

into STATIC_ROOT at all).

`alpha` just needs to be broadcasted to multiply with `self.act(F.negative(x))`, so just use `broadcast_mul` instead of `*`. So modifying @piiswrong's code we have:

```
class PReLU(gluon.HybridBlock):
    def __init__(self, *args):
        super(PReLU, self).__init__(*args)
        with self.name_scope():
            self.alpha = self.params.get('alpha', shape=(1,), init=mx.init.Zero())
            self.act = gluon.nn.Activation(activation='relu')

    def hybrid_forward(self, F, x, alpha):
        pos = self.act(x)
        neg = F.broadcast_mul(F.negative(alpha), self.act(F.negative(x)))
        return pos + neg
```

Right @mseeger  a unit test tests a unit of functionality independently, which will often correspond 1:1 to a class a function, or a single module, with any other modules and dependencies being mocked. If you are testing more than that, you should call it a functional test, an acceptance test, and end-to-end test or whatever, but don't call it a unit test. It's fine to have such tests, but they can run nightly, not in every PR. which is creating the situation that we have now.

`import argparse
import find_mxnet
import mxnet as mx
import time
import os, sys
import logging
import importlib
sys.path.insert(0, "./settings")
sys.path.insert(0, "../")

import logging
logger = logging.getLogger()
logger.setLevel(logging.INFO)

formatter = logging.Formatter('%(asctime)s - %(message)s')
console = logging.StreamHandler()
console.setFormatter(formatter)
logger.addHandler(console)

def get_fine_tune_model(sym, arg_params, num_classes, layer_name, batchsize):
    
    all_layers = sym.get_internals()
    net = all_layers[layer_name+'_output']
    net = mx.symbol.FullyConnected(data=net, num_hidden=num_classes, name='fc')
    net = mx.symbol.SoftmaxOutput(data=net, name='softmax')

    new_args = dict({k:arg_params[k] for k in arg_params if 'fc' not in k})
    return (net, new_args)

def multi_factor_scheduler(begin_epoch, epoch_size, step=[7,14], factor=0.1):
    step_ = [epoch_size * (x-begin_epoch) for x in step if x-begin_epoch > 0]
    return mx.lr_scheduler.MultiFactorScheduler(step=step_, factor=factor) if len(step_) else None

def train_model(model, gpus, batch_size, image_shape, epoch=0, num_epoch=20, kv='device'):
    train = mx.image.ImageIter(
        batch_size          = args.batch_size,
        data_shape          = (3,224,224),        
        label_width         = 1,
        path_imglist        = args.data_train,
        path_root           = args.image_train,
        part_index          = kv.rank,
        num_parts           = kv.num_workers,
        shuffle             = True,        
        data_name           = 'data',
        label_name          = 'softmax_label',
        aug_list            = mx.image.CreateAugmenter((3,224,224),resize=224,rand_crop=True,rand_mirror=True,mean=True,std=True))

    val = mx.image.ImageIter(
        batch_size          = args.batch_size,
        data_shape          = (3,224,224),
        label_width         = 1,
        path_imglist        = args.data_val,
        path_root           = args.image_val,
        part_index          = kv.rank,
        num_parts           = kv.num_workers,       
        data_name           = 'data',
        label_name          = 'softmax_label',
        aug_list            = mx.image.CreateAugmenter((3,224,224),resize=224,mean=True,std=True))

    kv = mx.kvstore.create(args.kv_store)

    prefix = model
    sym, arg_params, aux_params = mx.model.load_checkpoint(prefix, epoch)

    (new_sym, new_args) = get_fine_tune_model(
        sym, arg_params, args.num_classes, 'flatten', args.batch_size)

    epoch_size = max(int(args.num_examples / args.batch_size / kv.num_workers), 1)
    lr_scheduler=multi_factor_scheduler(args.epoch, epoch_size)

    optimizer_params = {
            'learning_rate': args.lr,
            'momentum' : args.mom,
            'wd' : args.wd,
            'lr_scheduler': lr_scheduler}
    initializer = mx.init.Xavier(
            rnd_type='gaussian', factor_type="in", magnitude=2)

    if gpus == '':
        devs = mx.cpu()
    else:
        devs = [mx.gpu(int(i)) for i in gpus.split(',')]
        
    model = mx.mod.Module(
        context       = devs,
        symbol        = new_sym
    )

    checkpoint = mx.callback.do_checkpoint(args.save_result)

    eval_metric = ['accuracy']

    model.fit(train,
              begin_epoch=epoch,
              num_epoch=num_epoch,
              eval_data=val,
              eval_metric=eval_metric,
              kvstore=kv,
              optimizer='sgd',
              optimizer_params=optimizer_params,
              arg_params=new_args,
              aux_params=aux_params,
              initializer=initializer,
              allow_missing=True,
              batch_end_callback=mx.callback.Speedometer(args.batch_size, 20),
              epoch_end_callback=checkpoint)
    
if __name__ == '__main__':
    parser = argparse.ArgumentParser(description='score a model on a dataset')
    parser.add_argument('--model',         type=str, required=True,)
    parser.add_argument('--gpus',          type=str, default='0')
    parser.add_argument('--batch-size',    type=int, default=200)
    parser.add_argument('--epoch',         type=int, default=0)
    parser.add_argument('--image-shape',   type=str, default='3,224,224')
    parser.add_argument('--data-train',    type=str)
    parser.add_argument('--image-train',   type=str)
    parser.add_argument('--data-val',      type=str)
    parser.add_argument('--image-val',     type=str)
    parser.add_argument('--num-classes',   type=int, default=6)
    parser.add_argument('--lr',            type=float, default=0.001)
    parser.add_argument('--num-epoch',     type=int, default=20)
    parser.add_argument('--kv-store',      type=str, default='device', help='the kvstore type')
    parser.add_argument('--save-result',   type=str, help='the save path')
    parser.add_argument('--num-examples',  type=int)
    parser.add_argument('--mom',           type=float, default=0.9, help='momentum for sgd')
    parser.add_argument('--wd',            type=float, default=0.0001, help='weight decay for sgd')
    args = parser.parse_args()

    logger = logging.getLogger()
    logger.setLevel(logging.DEBUG)

    kv = mx.kvstore.create(args.kv_store)

    if not os.path.exists(args.save_result):
        os.mkdir(args.save_result)
    hdlr = logging.FileHandler(args.save_result+ '/train.log')
    hdlr.setFormatter(formatter)
    logger.addHandler(hdlr)
    logging.info(args)

    train_model(model=args.model, gpus=args.gpus, batch_size=args.batch_size,
          image_shape='3,224,224', epoch=0, num_epoch=args.num_epoch, kv=kv)

Looks like it's not an official term but that's what I call them, a merge conflict where there are no conflicts on the same line (e.g. one commit changes x = 3 -> x = 4, and another changes x = 3 -> x = 5), but rather two commits that change the logic in different places and when combined cause a breakage (e.g. one commit x = 3 -> x = 4, and another adds a check to make sure that x is still 3 later on).

I'm guessing the goal of wiring this `verbose` flag around to silence logging? First do we need to silence logging? And if we do can we do that through the logging handler, maybe log to a file and print the progress bar?

If the flag suppresses logging in favor of [only] a bar I'm not sure if I'd use it as I'd loose touch on what is currently happening. Simply printing `5 out of 9 dag runs finished\n 455 out of 512 tasks successful` at every backfill heartbeat serves pretty much the same purpose without all this extra logic and hiding other potentially useful details.

This smart_create method is something I had in mind for a long time but I would not like to create separate one but rather extend the existing create method to know how to recognize what has been passed in the inputs. Just to be clear, this is not a feature that we would implement in place, we would always preserve the current behavior. To shed some light on why this is the behavior in the SDK vs the API directly: this is a limitation of how the service API is modeled. While calling the API directly can return a loosely structured JSON document (where a key may go to a `list[str]` or `str`), whereas the SDKs need to deserialize the response into a proper data structure, thus all keys must go to the same known type (in this case `list[str]`). 

If this ever were to be added as a feature we would add an additional higher level interface to preserve backwards compatibility.

74a061e87141c7f4615068461cad4b881ddff769 implements [the suggestion above](https://github.com/boto/botocore/pull/1157#issuecomment-304376764)

This change necessarily requires a PR against boto3 to update the [configuration documentation](https://github.com/boto/boto3/blob/develop/docs/source/guide/configuration.rst).

This uses two configuration settings, one to set the type of cache to be used, and one to set the directory to store the cache in the filesystem if a file based cache is used. This seeks to follow the intent of the `cache` argument to `credentials.AssumeRoleProvider` by leaving it open to additional cache systems being added down the road.

The default behavior is to function as botocore does currently by using an in memory cache.

The default file cache directory is a new directory, `~/.aws/cache`. This is different from the cache directory of awscli (`~/.aws/cli/cache`) but the user can set it to be the same so that botocore and awscli share cache files.

Feel free to suggest different configuration setting names or environment variable names, suggest words other than `memory` and `file` to select the cache system, suggest a different default directory or anything else.

I did not add a unit test for the session `conversion_func` values for `credential_cache` and `credential_cache_directory` which use `string.lower` and `os.path.exanduser` respectively because it felt like overkill but I'm happy to if it' needed.

The problem is that the generated stub has `Optional[str]` as the annotation for `url` param of `Data.__init__`, but your source code has just `str` for that annotation. This shouldn't be possible; we are supposed to always privilege the actual annotations in the source over the derived data from tracing. But somehow that's failing in this case.

The rst doc that @jab pointed to is used within a sphinx project with the same makefile etc. it's just the directives in that rst file referenced that specifically extract docstrings from the code.

Sphinx documentation is what is usually expected for a library within the Python community (It's the tool used for generating the core python documentation and so is well-recognised). The insertion of the reference docs isn't the end-goal; The end goal is a generated documentation site documenting the library, which would require writing some more rst.

If I remember correctly, all primary JSON types (string, number, array, object) are handled explicitly by `simplejson`, and `default` user function is never called. Default function is called only on unserializable types. `float` is usually serializable, and when it is, it should be serialized by `simplejson` (like other primary types are). Sometimes it's not (particularly when `allow_nan=False`, and strict adherence to JSON is requested). I'm proposing that in those cases - when `float` is not serializable - user supplied `default` function is called.

In that sense, this seems to be "a missing piece" in order to have consistent serialization of non-standard JSON types. Integer numbers, booleans, null, strings, lists and dicts (at least on container level) are always serializable. Floats are the only exception. (Inf/NaN in `Decimal` are indirectly handled by the same float processor.)

The use case? I was trying to support JSON-compatible serialization of non-finite floats in my library [`jsonplus`](https://github.com/randomir/jsonplus).

So, even worse. You see why this could use a line of documentation somewhere.

> The django-brambling repository is a project repository, not a reusable package. We use it to deploy our servers, so it's imperative that the Pipfile.lock is honored, even if we have git dependencies. 

Reusability and being a package don't have anything to do with one another, being reusable doesn't require being a package, and being a package doesn't imply that a thing is reusable.  Many teams use local packaging environments to install their own software in production or testing, or to insulate individual dependencies.

> In this case, our pipfile.lock correctly listed django 1.8.11 as the locked version, but django 1.11.12 was installed despite that.

I agree. That is a desirable outcome.

> More generally, I would also expect that explicitly stating a requirement for a particular version of a package would have precedence over all subdependencies of packages I might list, no matter what order things are listed in.

I actually disagree with this, lets use an example.  Say you install `Package A` and `Package B==1.0`.  And say for instance `Package A` has a strict pin itself depending on `Package B>=1.1`. You're suggesting some kind of weighting given to the dependency graph based on how close to the top a version is, but that doesn't really work because you may simply be installing a broken environment. We can't simply trust that your version is correct.

In this case your version (`1.8`) does satisfy the other dependencies, so it's just an issue of how the resolver itself works, which is that it finds the best satisfying dependency and moves on rather than storing the context (which is actually a difficult problem in and of itself).  We have an open issue that would allow for per-package overrides which helps with this kind of thing (#1921)

This is a resolution order issue and I agree it should be handled correctly. While I understand this may seem simple, in fact it isn't, or it would be working correctly :) That's why we try to let the resolver handle this instead of using strict pins.

> I'm not quite clear from your responses in this thread whether these are acknowledged as bugs or if they're seen as user error. It's pretty clear in my mind that they are bugs

They are most certainly bugs, that's why we have open issues addressing them and flattening the dependency graph is not something you will get out of pip either, which is also order-sensitive when installing `requirements.txt`. I am offering you paths to solve the problem while we nail down the resolution issues.

> My understanding of install_requires  has always been that it's intended for open-source packages, to ensure  that dependencies are automatically installed via pip, not for setting  up a virtual environment with specific packages for a deploy; since this  is for a project repo, I'm hesitant to use install_requires. 

`install_requires` has nothing to do with open source packages, in fact it has nothing to do with pip.  `setup.py` files are processed by setuptools, `install_requires` specifies dependencies to setuptools, and `setup.py` + the structure you're using inherently makes your project a package already.  When you put dependencies in `install_requires` it allows you to run `pip install -e .` aka installing your project in editable mode (which updates the installation as you work on it) or to just `pip install .` (or `pip install /path/to/directory/with/setupfile/`) -- the same works where you just replace `pip` with `pipenv` and it then resolves dependencies into the lockfile as well

The primary advantage of this is that it makes pipenv treat your project as _the only_ top level dependency and resolves everything relative to it.

> Are you  saying that install_requires is used by pipenv as a "base" for the  requirements listed in the two package sections? 

No, pipenv has nothing to do with `install requires`, other than respecting its relationship to python packaging in general.

> if I put django in [packages] at 1.8.11 and django-debug-toolbar in [dev-packages], I end up with django 2.0 installed, as a dependency of django-debug-toolbar.

On what version of pipenv? Dev packages should never win in resolution against packages, I'd be curious to see a pipfile that shows this happening

To provide a really practical example, I have a project at work where we do a few things.

 - setup.py gets necessary app dependencies
 - requirements.txt is used for defining an internal pypi and adding runtime deps (db driver to use)
 - dev_requirements.txt is used to install test/dev reqs like pytest, mock, webtest, etc.
 - ops_requirements.txt is used for a parallel operational venv that became necessary b/c a lib conflicted with the app requirements!

This project is for some integration so its requirements might be complicated than others, but generally, keeping app requirements you need in setup.py and runtime or deployment requirements in requirements.txt ends up helpful for the simple reason you can use pip flags in requirements.txt.

This is a bug in patchelf: it turns out that when you link against a library like libgfortran that uses symbol versioning, then the library gets listed in two different places: once as a DT_NEEDED, and once in the symbol version requirements table. patchelf --replace-needed fixes the DT_NEEDED entry, but doesn't touch the symbol version requirements table, so we have:
> 
> $ readelf -a lib/python2.7/site-packages/numpy/.libs/libopenblasp-r0-4830d766.2.17.so
> [...]
> Dynamic section at offset 0x2318100 contains 28 entries:
>   Tag        Type                         Name/Value
>  0x0000000000000001 (NEEDED)             Shared library: [libm.so.6]
>  0x0000000000000001 (NEEDED)             Shared library: [libpthread.so.0]
>  0x0000000000000001 (NEEDED)             Shared library: [libgfortran-ed201abd.so.3.0.0]
> [...]
> Version needs section '.gnu.version_r' contains 4 entries:
>  Addr: 0x00000000000aff18  Offset: 0x0aff18  Link: 28 (.dynstr)
>   000000: Version: 1  File: libgfortran.so.3  Cnt: 1
>   0x0010:   Name: GFORTRAN_1.0  Flags: none  Version: 7
> Notice that the first part references libgfortran-ed201abd.so.3.0.0, but the second part references libgfortran.so.3. And then this freaks out the dynamic loader, because it's being asked to check the symbol versions of a library that wasn't even loaded.

Right, basically the idea is to solve the ELF namespace collision problem once and for all by finding all the symbols exported by our vendored libraries and giving them unique names, and then finding all the imports of those symbols and patching them up to use the new unique names.

Basically this is one of those solutions that requires some ridiculous hoop-jumping, but in some sense it's the only *really* reliable solution, and it offends me that we can't do it. (It's just rewriting some lists of strings stored in an admittedly-rather-complicated data structure! who's in charge around here anyway?!)

I spent like... half an hour looking at this, anyway, and it isn't *obviously* untenable. The main obstacle that [previous attempts](https://sourceware.org/ml/binutils/2006-03/msg00266.html) ran into is that if you modify the size of the `.dynstr` section then you need to go through the whole file and fix up all the relative offsets but... patchelf is already solving this problem, because commands like `patchelf --replace-needed` (which we use now) also have to modify the size of `.dynstr`. That said, there's still some non-trivial work to do to identify all the places where exported/imported symbols are named, modify them, and then fix things up. (In particular you need to rebuild the hash tables or otherwise deal with them somehow.) Quite possibly there are other obstacles I haven't noticed, but it might be doable in a weekend?

There's also this thing, which might already be doing most of what we want, not sure: http://mit.edu/~jfc/src/sym-rename.c

CC'ing @geofft because somehow I have the impression that this is the kind of mad science heroism that might appeal to him. Could be wrong.

I see. The issue is that Setuptools is seeking specifically to reverse that dependency. The responsibility for installing packages is being delegated to pip, and that's a more fundamental responsibility than that of building packages, for which setuptools will play a key role.

In that sense, I'd recommend you build packages in this order - `python`, `pip`, `wheel`, `setuptools` (or maybe setuptools before wheel).

Strictly speaking, pip doesn't depend on setuptools. Setuptools is required to build some packages (from source), but not for installing wheels. Setuptools is an optional dependency.

Is it possible with this different perspective that conda-forge can be adapted to work in this new model?

Alternatively, conda-forge could be responsible for retrieving build dependencies and bootstrapping the project. If it makes sense to separate the two responsibilities that `bootstrap.py` is performing, that would be possible.

@akaptur Thanks for this super helpful bug report!  I'm pretty sure the behaviors you've observed are down two bugs in `trial`.

The first bug is that `trial` doesn't make it clear that a test error's cause is an `Exception` that was logged but not flushed by the end of a test.  [`trial` considers these to be errors](http://twistedmatrix.com/documents/17.5.0/api/twisted.trial.unittest.SynchronousTestCase.html#flushLoggedErrors), which is nice but needlessly confusing given the poor messaging around their reporting.

In your reproducing example, [`klein` logs unhandled errors](https://github.com/twisted/klein/blob/844d1d3d3a8cf67259848ef2d2fe6ccc309cf8fb/src/klein/resource.py#L289), so that's the source of the `[ERROR]`.  

[I've filed a ticket to improve reporting of errors that were logged but not flushed](https://twistedmatrix.com/trac/ticket/9266).  I think it's related because if Twisted _did_ explain that this `[ERROR]` came from an unflushed logging error, the second bug would have been more obvious.

That second bug explains the incorrect attribution of the `[ERROR]` that you identified.  It turns out that `SynchronousTestCase` doesn't flush logged errors if a test method raises an exception!  [Moving the log flushing code under the preceding `finally:`](https://github.com/twisted/twisted/blob/2ca7c76073e10a6694623c5e4bf934c3000786e6/src/twisted/trial/_synctest.py#L1359-L1364) fixed the attribution for me locally. I filed [a second ticket](https://twistedmatrix.com/trac/ticket/9267) to address this and a related problem.

Please update those two tickets if I missed something, and also please add yourself to the CC field.

Can you also move this issue to https://github.com/twisted/klein?  Even though the bugs are in Twisted proper, I'd like to verify that those Trac tickets actually fix this.

Thanks!

> They don't "lose timezone information", whatever that means. You're not giving timezone information to the now() function in the first place. Try this: datetime.datetime.now(tz=pytz.utc).

Of note, in Python 3 the maintainers added [a UTC instance to the standard library](https://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html#datetime.timezone.utc), and [built-in support for fixed-offset timezones](https://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html#timezone-objects). This support has also been added to strptime, so if you parse a datetime string with a fixed-time offset and the `%z` operator you'll get a zoned datetime:
```python
>>> datetime.strptime("2015-03-15T07:54:13-1045", "%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%S%z")
datetime.datetime(2015, 3, 15, 7, 54, 13, tzinfo=datetime.timezone(datetime.timedelta(-1, 47700)))
```
You still have to use pytz for "named" timezones.

Hi @johnathan-s,

Idempotency key is not needed for `GET` and `DELETE`; by definition the http methods are idempotent. So we shouldn't add it unnecessarily, but doing so will make no difference to results. The recommended way for generation is `V4 UUIDs`.

If I understand your suggestion about centralization of idempotency setting correctly, yup! we can supply the idempotency key in the header `request_headers` in file `api_requestor` where other Stripe-specific headers are dealt with; each underlying http-client doesn't have to take care of it. If the caller passes the key in `supplied_header` it will override our automatically-generated key. At this layer, we also has access to http method as well.

Hmm I don't think the specific underlying client is exposed outside the `HTTPClient` class, and therefore it isn't recorded in user-agent. Maybe there are other ways that I'm not aware of. I'll follow up with the team on this, if we should go ahead with `requests` specific implementation. And I do agree that if we use this client, the added randomness in delay is less of a concern.

Meanwhile, it would be great to have tests on the added features, idempotency key and the retries. One needs to install https://github.com/stripe/stripe-mock to run our pytests.

This is a weird one indeed!

It seems to be due to the fact that when no `indent` parameter is passed, the `json` module will [try to use its C encoder](https://github.com/python/cpython/blob/7c2fcc5c607f0ae22a8b0d77aeb98ca2fc7a760b/Lib/json/encoder.py#L246-L256) rather than its pure Python encoder.

For some reason, the C encoder trips when trying to encode our `ListObject`s:
```python
>>> lo = stripe.resource.ListObject.construct_from(
            {'object': 'list', 'data': [{'id': 'foo'}]},
            'mykey'
        )
>>> lo
<ListObject list at 0x103ca7178> JSON: {
  "data": [
    {
      "id": "foo"
    }
  ], 
  "object": "list"
}
>>> json.dumps(lo)

Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<pyshell#188>", line 1, in <module>
    json.dumps(lo)
  File "/Users/ob/.pyenv/versions/2.7.11/lib/python2.7/json/__init__.py", line 244, in dumps
    return _default_encoder.encode(obj)
  File "/Users/ob/.pyenv/versions/2.7.11/lib/python2.7/json/encoder.py", line 207, in encode
    chunks = self.iterencode(o, _one_shot=True)
  File "/Users/ob/.pyenv/versions/2.7.11/lib/python2.7/json/encoder.py", line 270, in iterencode
    return _iterencode(o, 0)
TypeError: keys must be a string
```
even though both keys are strings, and it's perfectly capable of encoding both values:
```python
>>> [type(k) for k in lo.keys()]
[<type 'str'>, <type 'str'>]
>>> json.dumps(lo['object'])
'"list"'
>>> json.dumps(lo['data'])
'[{"id": "foo"}]'
```

I cannot think of a simple way to fix this on our end. The workaround is pretty simple, just pass an `indent` parameter. Alternatively, it's possible to kill the C encoder:
```python
import json
json.encoder.c_make_encoder = None
```

Note that if you can live with `indent=2` and `sort_keys=True`, it's much simpler and cleaner to use the built-in JSON encoding, e.g. `str(customer)`.

:+1: for breaking API, but I strongly prefer to have a discussion first.

From my point of view, there are several considerations:

1. All functions on thresholding have to return the same kind of output, let's call it `THR`. Some of them may return tuple `(THR, ...)` if there is any important and/or useful extra information user would like to have;
2. `THR` has to be one of these two alternatives:

    A. integer/float which is used as _global_ threshold (`img > THR`), or numpy array of the same shape as input image (binarization also via `img > THR`) if the threshold values are computed using _local_ algorithms and therefore aren't always equal;
    B. copy of input image already thresholded (binary).
    I do prefer the first alternative, but we could let user choose between them using extra function argument. In any case, I think that returning the threshold number/array should be the preffered way (`return_threshold=True` by default).

Hi @TheShadow29 

I think the difference is much smaller than you think. One of the perils of working with uint8 instead of float! `abs(img12 - img2)` will result in negative values, anywhere `img12` is smaller than `img2`, looping back to 255. Try `diff = abs(img12.astype(float) - img2.astype(float)).astype('uint8')`, then compute the mean and max of that image.

I don't know that it's generally true that "it should be all in the integer domain when the input is of type uint8". The choice of `floor` in the paper you linked is a consequence of the desire to work in the integer domain. One workaround I've found that might apply to your issue is to simply move the `'rest_framework_jwt.authentication.JSONWebTokenAuthentication',` to the top like so: 

```
    REST_FRAMEWORK = {
        'DEFAULT_PERMISSION_CLASSES': (
            'rest_framework.permissions.IsAuthenticated',
        ),
        'DEFAULT_AUTHENTICATION_CLASSES': (
            'rest_framework_jwt.authentication.JSONWebTokenAuthentication',
            'rest_framework.authentication.SessionAuthentication',
            'rest_framework.authentication.BasicAuthentication',
        ),
    }
```

This causes the JWT to be considered first if present, and the session cookie is never looked at.

I haven't found this to cause any other issues, but I haven't used it in production yet. 

 [My SO Q&A on the issue](http://stackoverflow.com/questions/41494671/django-session-causes-angular-with-jwt-token-missing-or-incorrect-error/41494672#41494672)

I ended up solving this my overriding `authenticate_credentials` to look something like:
```python
    def authenticate_credentials(self, payload):
        User = utils.get_user_model()
        user_id = jwt_get_user_id_from_payload(payload)
        if user_id is not None:
            user, created = User.objects.get_or_create(user_id=user_id)
...
```

Here's an experiment I did:

```python
from __future__ import print_function
import gc
from sys import getrefcount

def demo(x):
   print('D1', getrefcount(x))
   locals()
   gc.collect()
   print('D2', getrefcount(x))

x = object()
print('O1', getrefcount(x))
demo(x)
print('O2', getrefcount(x))
```

This shows to me that all of the references ought to be cleaned up when the `locals()`-calling function in question wraps up. If that's not the case for you, I think that implies that Numexpr function has a leak.
