I understand your point but I don't believe it's a bug, I would call it  is a convention: the returns are reported at the timestamp at which the transaction happens (when the factor is traded, and new long or short positions are entered). To know when the return is actually realized we have to add to this timestamp the timedelta corresponding to the period for which the returns are computed for (1 hour, 1 day etc.) The function that computes the cumulative returns behaves consistently with this convention, It doesn't seem to be buggy.

Of course we can change this convention is we like, but I believe this decision has a historical reason. The main Alphalens use case is to analyze factors computed at market open and the daily returns are better shown on the same day the factor is traded because the returns accumulate during the trading day. Reporting the returns the following day, when the returns are actually realized would seem strange. If the common scenario was to analyze factors computed just before market close, I believe a different convention would have been chosen.

> we don't want to increase the computation required for the common scenario of a single factor

`fwdret = prices.pct_change(period).shift(-period).reindex(factor_idx)` forward returns is actually computed for all prices dates, and then part of it discarded. It is the time for stacking data frames and combing columns that is saved, rather than that for computing forward returns. I understand that It can be significantly more expensive to concatenate columns than computing pct_changes. In this case I agree that there is a performance gain.

But when it comes to the multi-factor analysis case, if the length of the forward return data frame depends on the factor, the user has to carefully choose a factor to compute forward returns so that the forward returns can be reused in other analysis, since custom factor algorithms may introduce unpredictable number of missing values.

Maybe it does not need to subset the forward returns frame in `compute_forward_returns`. Aligning factor and forward_returns is eventually handled in `get_clean_factor(factor, forward_returns)`.

It seems to be a trade-off here. Personally, I think in the single factor scenario forward returns are computed only once, so the overhead is acceptable, and it may not often be significant. On the other hand, of course, multi-factor users can, in a tricky way, get the full-date forward returns by passing the prices itself as a factor.

> We don't know if prices is correctly populated, it might miss some dates from factor. To be correct in the computation we perform a union of the two indices instead of relying on the correctness of prices
1. In practice, I assume that pricing data, as raw data, is less likely to omit any trading dates, relative to factors computed with custom algorithms. This is reliable at least in my local market. Is this assumption so weak in other markets that we have to pay special attention?

2. In terms of the code, `full_idx = factor_idx.union(prices_idx)`, where `factor_idx` has already become the result of the intersection between `factor_idx` and `prices_idx`, effectively a subset of `prices_idx`. The union of a subset and `prices_idx` itself always yields `prices_idx` itself. In other words, `factor_idx` does not matter here. Am I missing anything?

Are you using Zappa? Had the same issue, fix was...

`"transport": "raven.transport.requests.RequestsHTTPTransport"` in your RAVEN_CONFIG.

In your `zappa_settings.json` you want `"app_function": "project.wsgi"` going to your Django `wsgi.py` file.

And in `wsgi.py` you want something like this:

```
import os

from django.core.wsgi import get_wsgi_application
from raven.middleware import Sentry

os.environ.setdefault("DJANGO_SETTINGS_MODULE", "project.settings")

application = Sentry(get_wsgi_application())
```

This check is simply incorrect for [rdf:List](https://www.w3.org/TR/2004/REC-rdf-schema-20040210/#ch_collectionvocab) that is quite unrestricted. Returning to non-frozen-flow...  Right, one can reset the rng to make consistent time-integrations.  What I worry about in that case is just speed.  Updating a non-frozen-flow layer requires 2 FFTs per time-step, which get pretty expensive if the layer is large enough to subtend a significant FOV at high altitude.  Frozen-flow is cheaper since you just query a different piece of a static screen at each time step.  My thought was that non-frozen-flow might still be possible if we could avoid recomputing the atmosphere for each PSF.

Here are some estimates for a whole focal plane simulation for LSST:

A 3.5 degree field subtends about 1 km at 15 km altitude.  If we take the resolution to be r0-ish (~15 cm) for now, then the highest screen is ~6000 pixels across.  If we add slop for wind, then 8192 pixels seems natural.  My laptop takes ~6.6 s to FFT an 8192x8192 array using np.fft.  That times 6 layers and 500 time steps (30 ms steps for 15 sec) makes ~5.5 hours overhead for non-frozen-flow that wouldn't be required with frozen-flow.  If the inner loop is PSFs, then we only eat this once though.

Let's estimate the PSF generation too:  the 8.4 m aperture extends 56 15-cm samples.  We want padding for the FFT though, so let's say turning phase into a PSF is a 256x256 FFT, which costs np.fft 1.74 ms.  That times 500 time steps makes 875 ms per PSF, not including time to look up the particular 56x56 patch required for each step.  (BTW, I've been playing with using `InterpolatedImage` and `drawImage` for this, though really I just want a 2D version of `LookupTable`.  Maybe we should make one!)  For 50 objects per arcmin^2 and 9.6 sq. deg., we get 420 hours per LSST focal plane (for just the PSF FFTs).

It's still a research question, of course, what makes a reasonable time step and resolution...

Indeed, this is different a  image - the e-passport  "Chip Inside symbol " is actually what is printed on the cover of a passport booklet to indicate that the passport contains a chip and can be read electronically. A passport with this sign printed on the cover is thus an e-passport usable in automated e-gate or kiosk systems at airport for example (for biometric recognition, from the electronic face image or the fingerprint images stored in the chip). This is an standardized ICAO sign to denote such passport  See http://www.icao.int/publications/Documents/9303_p9_cons_en.pdf , page 2 & 3. In particular, this sign is used in signage at airport to indicate e-passport usage.

Adding of a new endpoints to the API is absolutely not a problem. Together with SSL and Basic-Auth support I've implemented simple routing mechanism. Original python module was routing all GET requests to `do_GET` method, POST requests to `do_POST` method and so on. The new routing is a little bit smarter:
`GET /foo/bar` request will call `do_GET_foo` method.

For us this means that we just need to implement bunch of new methods:
```python
    @check_auth
    def do_POST_shutdown(self):
        """handler for `POST /shutdown` request"""

    @check_auth
    def do_POST_promote(self):
        """handler for `POST /promote` request"""
```

`@check_auth` decorator is taking care about authorisation for a given endpoint.

**ttl** - sets when leader key should expire if there are no updates. Default value 30 seconds
**loop_wait** -- how often Patroni should run HA loop. Default value 10 seconds.
**retry_timeout** -- if update of the leader key failed, Patroni will retry it during this time. Default value 10 seconds.
**maximum_lag_on_failover** -- every iteration of HA loop, leader writes into etcd its wal_position. In case of leader race every replica will compare its wal_position with the last registered wal_position of the leader in etcd. If the difference is more than maximum_lag_on_failover, it wont promote.

If Patroni can't communicate with etcd and postgres was running as master, it will restart postgres in read-only.

@Tinche I like your updates to the setup script - especially overriding the install command to include the `pyrobuf_list` and `pyrobuf_util` modules as extensions included with the main distribution rather than in a separate package. You'll need to update the `generate_list` and `list_and_util` commands as they rely on `_pre_install` and `_post_install` methods which no longer exist.

Interestingly, the `pyrobuf_list.pyx` and `pyrobuf_list.pxd` are included in the pyrobuf distribution so the jinja2 step is technically unnecessary. I wouldn't change it though.

A disadvantage of using `setup_requires` for us is that our network is behind a firewall that blocks access to pypi. We have our own local pypi cache and use `pip.conf` files to direct pip to use it. But `setup_requires` uses easy_install, which ignores the `pip.conf` file.

Nevertheless I think we should try to merge these changes ASAP. I'm really interested to see the `pyrobuf_modules` stuff you've done aswell.

>@fpagnoux To be clear what's the point with Reform vs TaxBenefitSystem? A Reform is an instance whereas a TaxBenefitSystem is a class?

Not even. A `Reform` is a particular case of a `TaxBenefitSystem` that is built by slightly modifying an existing `TaxBenefitSystem`.

In this case, they are functionally equivalent. We can consider that `france_data` is a reformed version of `france`, or just that it's another country package. Making it a reform allows to do comparative  simulations with and without the reform, but I doubt it is our usecase here.

I'm trying to remember the exact issues, but when I added the separate class, it was because there were some fundamental differences with the data structure and files at the time. This might be able to be revisited now that cfradial and Sigmet files are used by the aircraft  and @jjhelmus has done an awesome job to update the read routines to support extended headers, etc. 

And the elephant that I rarely acknowledge is that the class *only* supports tail Doppler radar data. Other airborne radar types cannot be read and/or visualized with this class currently. That is why the name change may be adopted. That way, there could be a call back to other components for different radar types (for example the belly radar on some aircraft behaves more like a surface-based system, while other aircraft have up to 6 antenna!)

Adam,

This is generally not possible.  Definitely not with hinted fonts.

I've heard @readroberts say a couple of times that they treat subroutines like individual glyphs.  While for some simple purposes that's possible, in general it's not.  You cannot even parse a subroutine into it's operands and operators on its own.  That is because if the subroutine (or any subroutine it calls, recursively) has a hintmask operator, you wouldn't know how many mask bytes follow.  The only way to know that is to have interpretted a specific glyph from the beginning.

For the same reason, you cannot have a hinted component in a subroutine that can be called from multiple glyphs, unless all those glyphs have very similar hints, which in general is not the case.

@oena brings up a good alternate structure. I did a side-by-side of a few common tasks that we might want to do with a GMT to see how they stack up.

```py
"""
Option 1: the old way

A GMT is stored as a list of dictionaries.
Each line is its own dictionary.
Each dictionary has the following keys:
    - head (string): identifier for the set
    - desc (string): longer description of the set
    - entries (list): members of the set


Option 2: proposed new way

A GMT is stored as a dictionary, where the keys are ids, and the values are
dictionaries. The values could also be tuples, but I think dictionary is
better so that you can refer to fields by name, rather than remembering
what's what in the tuple.

The nested dictionaries have the following keys:
	- desc (string): longer description of the set
       - entries (list): members of the set
"""

gmt1 = [{"head": "A", "desc": "this one is A", "entry": ["a1", "a3", "a2"]},
        {"head": "B", "desc": "this one is B", "entry": ["b4", "b2", "b3"]}]
gmt2 = {"A": {"desc": "this one is A", "entry": ["a1", "a3", "a2"]},
        "B": {"desc": "this one is B", "entry": ["b4", "b2", "b3"]}}

# Get names of sets
print [g["head"] for g in gmt1]
print gmt2.keys()

# Get descriptions of sets
print [g["desc"] for g in gmt1]
print [g["desc"] for g in gmt2.itervalues()]

# Get entries in sets
print [g["entry"] for g in gmt1]
print [g["entry"] for g in gmt2.itervalues()]

# Get lengths of sets
print [len(g["entry"]) for g in gmt1] # [g["length"]) for g in gmt1]
print [len(g["entry"]) for g in gmt2.itervalues()] # [g["length"]) for g in gmt2.itervalues()]

# Sort each set and return result as dict
dict1 = {}
for g in gmt1:
	dict1[g["head"]] = sorted(g["entry"])
print dict1

dict2 = {}
for set_name, set_rest in gmt2.iteritems():
	dict2[set_name] = sorted(set_rest["entry"])
print dict2
```

N.B. @tnat1031 , you'll notice that in this comparison, I have not introduced 'length' as another field for two reasons: 1) it's quite easy to extract length using the 'entry' field, and 2) I can see annoyances down the line in having to update the 'length' field if/when the 'entry' field is updated (and not worrying about whether the two are in sync doesn't seem like a good move either). But I included in the comments what the syntax would be if we did include it as a field.

Let me know what you all think.

If you are creating the security group in your troposphere script via CloudFormation then you should not use Parameter for it. That is only used for actual input from the CloudFormation console. You want to add it as a resource instead. The general form if you're in a VPC (and needing a group id) would be:

```
...
instanceSecurityGroup = t.add_resource(
    SecurityGroup(
        'InstanceSecurityGroup',
        GroupDescription='Enable SSH access via port 22',
        SecurityGroupIngress=[
            SecurityGroupRule(
                IpProtocol='tcp',
                FromPort='22',
                ToPort='22',
                CidrIp=Ref(sshlocation_param)),
            SecurityGroupRule(
                IpProtocol='tcp',
                FromPort='80',
                ToPort='80',
                CidrIp='0.0.0.0/0')],
        VpcId=Ref(VPC),
    ))
...
```
This is from examples/VPC_single_instance_in_subnet.py and note the Ref(VPC) which is a reference to a previously defined VPC object. This will cause CloudFormation to create a new resource. Then you can  reference it later on using `Ref(instanceSecurityGroup)`.

There is a little "magic" going on where the Ref in troposphere is to a python object which will output the appropriate security group name you specified when you created the object. This in turn will be used inside of CloudFormation to insert the appropriate group id.

In the Pants project we don't use pull requests for reviewing or submitting changes. Mostly because github's tooling around code reviews is pretty bad. We have a separate process for that (see below). The only thing we use pull requests for is to trigger CI builds, so we can confirm that the change isn't broken.

No one tracks the list of pull requests - we expect people to delete them as soon as their change is merged or discarded. I occasionally go through and delete old ones, which is what happened here.

If this change is still relevant then you can follow the process here to get it reviewed and merged: https://pantsbuild.github.io/dev.html.

@szha is right. You need to `flush()` before `close()`. In fact, you don't need to flush explicitly as `close()` would call `flush()` first before actually closing the file handle. The following code works for me.

```python
from mxboard import SummaryWriter


sw = SummaryWriter('./logs')
sw.add_text('test', 'test', 0)
sw.add_text('test', 'test', 1)
sw.close()
```

From the Prometheus [docs](https://prometheus.io/docs/practices/naming/):

> CAUTION: Remember that every unique key-value label pair represents a new time series, which can dramatically increase the amount of data stored. Do not use labels to store dimensions with high cardinality (many different label values), such as user IDs, email addresses, or other unbounded sets of values.

So maybe you have a point.  My approach of having per process metrics would require to have the pid number or some such as a label, which seems to be a against the recommendation above.  

OTOH, processes sharing data via a db file is a terrible solution, as well as any WSGI-specific solution (we have not only gunicorn workers, but also celery workers, and even simple multiprocessing module subprocesses).

Unfortunately I can't think of any good, efficient, and simple solution.  Here's an idea I just had, but take it more as a thought process than a finished idea:
  - Each worker tries to bind its well known fixed http port;
  - If it successfully binds, he becomes the representative of all the workers and will be scraped by Prometheus;
  - If it cannot bind that port, it means another worker has become representative first, so, instead, it will bind a random port, and then register with the representative worker telling it "hey, I am another sibling worker, and here's my port";
  - Whenever Prometheus sends an HTTP request to scrape the main worker, it knows how many "siblings" it has and so, scrapes each sibling as well.  It aggregates the siblings' metrics with its own metrics, before returning them to Prom.
  - To allow for the representative worker to die and be replaced, as well as additional workers dying, each sibling worker will attempt to become main worker periodically: bind the fixed port and becomes main worker, or else register itself with the main worker again.  The main worker removes from its list of siblings the other workers that have not registered with it in a long time.

I think this solution scales pretty well, since aggregation of data is done only at each scrape interval, not for every sample.  The downside is that it is a bit complicated. :(

@mikix thanks for the feedback. I have fixed the issues (and made a much simpler version really).

With regards to your comments:

E) This was the real problem. It is not a race per-se. But when you are done with a port, you need to jump through a few hoops to make sure it is free for future use and since we want to start a server on **the same** port, this had to be fixed.
E) the way I understand the code, un-subcribing in a thread will not solve anything. It is not the un-subscriptions that we were waiting on, it was the restarting of the server (see D). As already discussed, if you know that there is a network change, you know that the un-subscribe will block for a long time without achieving anything and so you can just set the timeout arbitrarily short (say 0.001) and it should actually unsubscribe faster than if we had still been connected. In the new version I have, I actually demand that all subscriptions has been cleared before stopping, so in this case, you cannot stop them in a thread. Just set the timeout really short.

With regards to:
>Looking into what is actually blocking... Seems that urlopen is one of the calls that is causing a block. Adding timeout=0 to it makes it not block anymore, as you'd expect (I don't know if that's a practical value in other cases -- it's not like we care about an answer to that call though).
>
>Then I hit the _listener_thread.join() call which blocks... Surely there's a way for socketserver.TCPServer to notice when network is dead...

Yes, that is what is blocking. The problem however is not that it is blocking, the problem is that the request never lands. Notice that the server is in loop where it is waiting for requests, and only after receiving each request will it check if it has been asked to shut down (``EventServerThread.run``). one way to fix this, is to set the ``handle_request`` up with a timeout. We do not want to do that for the general case though, as it makes the while run through an iteration every timeout, which is needless overhead, so therefore you will have to do it yourself.

I have made a new version in the same branch (currently at commit 90450ee98b). It is much simpler (less invasive to the code) and this now works for me:
```python

from __future__ import print_function
try:
    from queue import Empty
except:  # Py2.7
    from Queue import Empty

import soco
from pprint import pprint
from soco import events

# pick a device at random
device = soco.discover().pop()
print(device.player_name)
sub = device.renderingControl.subscribe()
events.set_event_listener_timeout(0.1)
print('Event server now running. Press ctrl-c to reset')

while True:
    try:
        event = sub.events.get(timeout=0.5)
        pprint(event.variables)
    except Empty:
        pass
    except KeyboardInterrupt:
        # If you suspect a network change, you can make this as short as you
        # want
        sub.unsubscribe(0.1)
        break

events.stop_event_listener()

sub = device.renderingControl.subscribe()
print('Event server now running again.')

while True:
    try:
        event = sub.events.get(timeout=0.5)
        pprint(event.variables)
    except Empty:
        pass
    except KeyboardInterrupt:
        sub.unsubscribe(0.1)
        break
```

The important parts are ``events.set_event_listener_timeout(0.1)``. Two important things:
 1. This call must be made AFTER making at least one subscription, so that the event server is running
 2. Must be called after each (re)-start of the event server (This could be fixed, if it turns out to be a large in-convenience, but first I want to see if it works.

Afterwards simple do `events.stop_event_listener()`, which will stop the event server. It will be automatically restarted when making a new subscription. NOTE that all the old subscriptions must be un-subscribed first. Use the timeout to do that fast.

0.0.0.0 is what gunicorn listens on, to say "I'll accept traffic from any interface", but it's not what you want to visit to get to the service from your browser. You need to connect to the IP of the container. Docker makes this annoyingly difficult. First find the container:

```
docker ps
```

Then find the IP for the container, using its container id:

```
docker inspect -f '{{range .NetworkSettings.Networks}}{{.IPAddress}}{{end}}' <container_id>
```

Then connect to that IP address.

In #50 I mentioned that we're recommending setting the FORWARDED_ALLOW_IPS environment var when running the docker image, because it's difficult to modify the setting if we set it by default in the image. We may followup later and have the docker image run a script as a command where we have a bit more flexibility in how we start the process in docker.

To see textual presentation use:

To subscribe to multicast stream use following code (from examples):

```
import socket
import struct
import asterix

sock = socket.socket(socket.AF_INET, socket.SOCK_DGRAM, socket.IPPROTO_UDP)
sock.setsockopt(socket.SOL_SOCKET, socket.SO_REUSEADDR, 1)
sock.bind(('', 21111))
mreq = struct.pack("=4sl", socket.inet_aton("232.1.1.11"), socket.INADDR_ANY)
sock.setsockopt(socket.IPPROTO_IP, socket.IP_ADD_MEMBERSHIP, mreq)

counter=1

while True:
    asterix_packet = sock.recv(10240)
    parsed = asterix.parse(asterix_packet)
    print('%d. Receiver received = %s' % (counter, parsed))
    counter += 1
```

For what it's worth, this is my solution to the problem (and a huge shoutout to @athoune for his answer which helped me). Named ```docker_call``` after our favorite ```subprocess``` function, it does virtually the same thing as ```Client.execute``` except it returns the return code instead if ```stream=True``` it will print the output of ```stdout``` and ```stderr``` to the console:
```python
def docker_call(cmd, docker_client, container_name, smart_replace=True, verbose=True):
    if smart_replace:
        cmd = do_smart_replace(cmd)
    if isinstance(cmd, six.string_types):
        cmd = shlex.split(str(cmd))
    data = {
        'Container': container_name,
        'User': '',
        'Privileged':False,
        'Tty': False,
        'AttachStdin': False,
        'AttachStdout': verbose,
        'AttachStderr': verbose,
        'Detach': False,  #Might need this option in the future.
        'Cmd': cmd
    }

    url = docker_client._url('/containers/{0}/exec'.format(container_name))
    res = docker_client._post_json(url, data=data)
    docker_client._raise_for_status(res)
    cmd_id = res.json().get('Id')
    res = docker_client._post_json(docker_client._url('/exec/{0}/start'.format(cmd_id)), data=data, stream=verbose)
    docker_client._raise_for_status(res)
    for i in docker_client._multiplexed_buffer_helper(res):
        logging.debug(i)
    url = docker_client._url('/exec/{0}/json'.format(cmd_id))
    res = docker_client._get(url)
    docker_client._raise_for_status(res)
    return res.json().get('ExitCode')
```

For reference, i'm quoting below the latest version of Adobe Feature File spec as available on the github afdko repository (the one published [on Adobe's website](https://www.adobe.com/devnet/opentype/afdko/topic_feature_file_syntax.html#2.f.i) is not up to date apparently) Source: https://github.com/adobe-type-tools/afdko/blob/develop/docs/OpenTypeFeatureFileSpecification.html ======

2.f.i. Glyph name

There are two different contexts for glyph naming: final production names and development names.

For production glyph names, names that are used in shipping font files, the specification is set by the Postscript and Type 1 specifications, which define what is expected by existing PostScript interpreters. These limitations are as follows.

A glyph name may be up to 63 characters in length, must be entirely comprised of characters from the following set:

A-Z a-z 0-9 . (period) _ (underscore)

and must not start with a digit or period. The only exception is the special character ".notdef".

"twocents", "a1", and "_" are valid glyph names. "2cents" and ".twocents" are not.

Development glyph names, names used in source data during the development of a font, have a larger supported character set. In addition to the characters allowed for production glyph names, the following characters must also be supported for development glyph names:

  • U+002A * Asterisk
  • U+002B + Plus sign
  • U+002D - Hyphen-minus
  • U+003A : Colon
  • U+005E ^ Circumflex accent
  • U+007C | Vertical bar
  • U+007E ~ Tilde
It looks like fontTools.subset can drop development names pretty easily, but only for TTFs until https://github.com/behdad/fonttools/issues/147 is resolved.

On Mar 13, 2017, 7:44 AM -0700, Casper van der Wel <notifications@github.com>, wrote:

While implementing different linking strategies for the FindLinker in #414<https://github.com/soft-matter/trackpy/pull/414>, I found this bug.

Apparently, only the numba subnetlinker handles the penalty for breaking links correctly. Both the recursive and nonrecursive strategies fail in this testcase. This testcase describes the following two source particles (o) and destination particles (x) with respective coordinates (0, 0), (8, 0), (7, 8), (15, 8).


o    o

    x    x



We have two likely linking results:

  1.  two links, total squared displacement = 2*(8**2 + 7**2) = 226
  2.  one link, total squared displacement = (8**2 + 1**2) + search_range**2

Case 2 gets a penalty for not linking, which equals the search range squared. Depending on the value of search_range, you expect different results:

  *   With a penalty of 13, case 2 has a total cost of 234 and we expect case 1. as the result.
  *   With a penalty of 12, case 2. will have a total cost of 209 and we expect case 2. as the result.

I am not sure what is the desired behaviour of the nonrecursive linker, but the recursive linker should for sure reproduce the result, and it does not.

@vitorbaptista @roll this is a more general issue as I understand it that the current library does not compute relative paths for resources correctly given the source url.

This came up in the JS implementation. The DataPackage object needs to check the location where it was originally initiated and then use that in computing the paths for resources which are relative to that original location.

The reason the data.okfn.org link works is that `url` is present on each resource (in fact violating the spec because both path and url are present - this is an overhang from an old hack on data.okfn.org). If we did not have url the code would fail.

If this analysis is correct I suggest we rename this issue to the more precise e.g.: "Handle path on resources not just url"

I think you'd have to find any instance of `etcdctl` in any of our scripts and add the `ca-file`, `cert-file`, and `key-file` flags. Because many of our components use etcdctl inside their /bin/boot scripts, I'd think that you'd need to custom-build the components themselves and somehow pass through your cert and key, maybe bind-mounting the file in the container. 

Simply put, it's a large undertaking. Thinking off the top of my head, a reasonable approach would be to propose a PR that makes all uses of `etcdctl` use an environment variable called `$ETCD_OPTS` or something, which could be passed into the running container. So, for example, the publish loop in a component changes from:
```
until etcdctl --no-sync -C $ETCD ls >/dev/null 2>&1; do
```
to
```
until etcdctl $ETCD_OPTS --no-sync -C $ETCD ls >/dev/null 2>&1; do
```

It's fine if it's blank, but users could add additional options.

So, yesterday I started to think a bit more about this PR after seeing #2976 (Add enforceHttp support), and I found a problem with this PR...

```
GET / HTTP/1.1
Host: http-headers.dev.deiscluster.com
User-Agent: Mozilla/5.0 (Windows NT 6.1; WOW64) AppleWebKit/537.36 (KHTML, like Gecko) Chrome/40.0.2214.93 Safari/537.36
Connection: close
Accept: text/html,application/xhtml+xml,application/xml;q=0.9,image/webp,*/*;q=0.8
Accept-Encoding: gzip, deflate, sdch
Accept-Language: no,en-US;q=0.8,en;q=0.6,nb;q=0.4,en-GB;q=0.2
Cache-Control: max-age=0
Connection: close
Cookie: [Cookies]
X-Forwarded-For: 84.213.0.0, 10.0.0.83
X-Forwarded-Port: 443
X-Forwarded-Proto: https
```

This is a domain running on https, and as we see, `X-Forwarded-Proto` is exposed saying that the connection is over https, which is awesome.

However, with Proxy protocol enabled, Nginx has no way of actually knowing that the original request was over HTTPS (since that part isn't exposed in the Proxy protocol). Some frameworks (Spring for instance) include ways to redirect HTTP -> HTTPS, and detect X-Forwarded-Proto and use that to enable HSTS, activate secure cookies and so forth.

Now, a workaround to that would be to expose a extra port, or to make nginx listen to 443 but with no SSL certificates (since I want that to terminate at the AWS ELB) and then make nginx send the `X-Forwarded-Proto` headers. I fancy neither of these solutions to be honest.

I think my preferred workaround for the whole websocket issue is to create a seperate ELB for the websocket endpoint, expose the websocket endpoint on a seperate subdomain and then keep on dreaming Amazon fixes ELB WebSocket support instead of releasing new features every month. 

To make this ELB follow the autoscaling it needs to be created at the same time the autoscaling group is created. I.e. as part of the `cloudformation json`.

This is how the **ELB feature matrix** looks: 
![http://i.imgur.com/pVCdZ2P.png](http://i.imgur.com/pVCdZ2P.png)

Now, based on the Matrix one might think - _the best solution for now is two ELBs, one in HTTP mode and one in TCP mode with Proxy protocol enabled,_ but alas it's not that easy...If NGINX is set to serve Proxy requests then it will do that. Any non-proxy requests would be denied.

This too can be solved, of course, but it will increase the complexity of the router and require exposing more ports.

My **recommendation** is to drop this PR and then I'll create a new one with a `cloudformation.json` with two ELBs (configurable of course), one in TCP mode and one in HTTP mode, as well as documentation on managing websockets.

It is true that if a user performs a `conda install B`, it will fail to get `B.2` because of the dependency limits, and there will be no notification that this has occurred. It is important to recognize, however, that while this may be new behavior _in this particular scenario_, it is something that _can and does happen now_ when installing a combination of packages. For example, consider
```
conda create -n test2 -c ipyrad ipyrad ipython
```
Once this _finally_ completes, it will present you with a nice recipe that includes IPython 4.0.0, and _not_ the latest version, IPython 4.0.3. And it will not tell you that it has been forced to install a less-than-latest version.

You're running into a common problem with bash.  Bash sees

    conda install astropy>1.2

as

    conda install astropy > 1.2

so bash is piping stdout to a file named `1.2`.  In this case you just need to use quotes.

    conda install 'astropy>1.2'

Nothing here for conda to fix really.  Just how bash works.

I understand the concern about "shadowing" executables. I think this is a basic skill command line users are just going to have to learn though.  Get bitten by it once, and learn.  Learn to use `which -a` and `type -a`.  The reason is that this isn't just something that involves conda.  It's a more general pattern of how OSes and PATHs work.

Even if I use homebrew, for example, to install python.  I now have on path both /usr/local/bin/python and /usr/bin/python.  I have on path two pips.  One I must use sudo with, one I absolutely shouldn't.  The shadowing argument, to me, is a fundamental user education issue, and not one we should try to patch around with non-standard hackiness.

I used to experience this exact same issue.  And I complained about it last week with Travis in the room.  Although I didn't understand the root cause.  After reading through these issues I think I might.

I've since done some more poking around, and here's what *does* work.  If you expect conda to be a system level package manager like yum or apt-get, you have to treat it like one from the beginning.  That is, install it as `root` and always mutate system state as `root`.  In fact, an install with `/bin/bash Miniconda-latest-Linux-x86_64.sh -bfp /usr/local` is working great so far.

What conda doesn't do is fall back gracefully to a user-writable location, especially for interacting with conda envs, when it can't write to the default install location.  If I install conda at `/usr/local` as root, and then as my own user try to create an env in my project directory, any type of caching and stuff should just happen at `~/.conda/pkgs`, etc.

Thanks for the bug report.  This is still an experimental aspect of the API and a known problem.  The basic issue is that removing header metadata and processing records that require it will crash the Python interpreter.  This is due to the underlying htslib library, which fails with a segmentation fault instead of simply generating an error when deleted metadata is accessed in a record.  We're working on approaches to fix this.

This error comes if the parser cannot convert a sequence bytes to a string using the cp1252 encoding. cobc (or ld.exe) seems to output something that is not decodable using the locale encoding. The differences between the compiler and the linter commands is that the linter command use ``-fsyntax-only``. So both commands are not invoking the exact same program and it is possible that e.g. the linker output some un-decodable character.

In case what I found is useful for someone upgrading and getting this change (or for any in the future who are doubtful about it) I still want to mention a few findings...

I tested a cronjob on Ubuntu 15.04 Vivid and RHEL7 that ran the date command and got results counter to what @dominikmueller saw. As expected it just printed the date to stdout, but I still got the cron emails. So it is likely that Curator will continue to email after this change if the output is not being redirected away. Obviously this may not hold true on some older releases or other distros, but it is better than the idea that this change will silence most people's output by default (which would be rather scary indeed).

There is also Chuck Houpt's Cronic (http://habilis.net/cronic/) and my spiritual successor to it "Foilhat" (repo is on this account) which can help you only get mail when there is output to stderr or the return code is non-zero.

Finally, for any still uncertain I wanted to share a trivia (and there are many) that rsync prints usage to stderr when there are no arguments but prints almost identical usage output to stdout when the help flag is used (i.e.: because you asked them for help, the usage output is no longer an error).

Could you please elaborate why running `az account set` is dangerous? My understanding is that even if we provide a `--subscription` parameter we will just set the subscription internally using the same flow of `az account set` since the Azure resource is under a subscription by design. For scripting purpose it's a matter of running either 2 commands or 1 command. As you also mentioned this is indeed a framework pattern that subscription is always required from users.

`az acr login` internally calls `docker login` but it requires the correct subscription to find the resource and obtain login credentials. Once you have the credentials, users could call `docker login` directly too (e.g., using admin user credentials, service principals, or AAD tokens).

Ok here is the pipeline ready to work with Gstreamer 1.0

Calling the set_property("configured", True) leads to a segfault
Not calling this line make me unable to use my custom dict, hmm and lmname, lmctl file. 

i hope you will find an answer. 

To try this pipeline, you should change all my default files to yours. You should also export the GST_PLUGIN_PATH to the GStreamer 1.0 install.
I hope it will help you ! Tell me if I can help you.

''' Python
#!/usr/bin/env python
# -*- coding: utf-8 -*-

import os, gi, sys

gi.require_version('Gst', '1.0')
from gi.repository import Gst
from gi.repository import GObject

GObject.threads_init()
Gst.init(None)

class Pipeline():
    def __init__(self): 
        """ some file to configure the pipe """               
        self.__dic__   = "/home/benoit/Bureau/Projet/VOXGenerator/test/sphinx/dictionnary.dic"
        self.__hmm__   = "/home/benoit/Bureau/Projet/VOXGenerator/test/sphinx/hmm"
        self.__lmctl__ = "/home/benoit/Bureau/Projet/VOXGenerator/test/lm/lmctl.txt"

        """ very simple pipe """
        self.__pipeline__ = Gst.parse_launch('alsasrc ! audioconvert ! audioresample ! pocketsphinx name=asr ! fakesink')
            
        """ the lmname """ 
        self.__lm__    = "Desktop"
        
        self.__loop__ = GObject.MainLoop()
        self.__previoushyp__ = ""
        
        """ grab the pocketsphinx element 
            without putting asr.set_property("configured", True) all other set_property have no effect on the pocketsphinx
            but calling asr.set_property("configured", True) lead to a segfault 
        """        
        asr = self.__pipeline__.get_by_name('asr')
        asr.set_property('hmm',    self.__hmm__)
        asr.set_property('dict',   self.__dic__)
        asr.set_property('lmctl',  self.__lmctl__)
        asr.set_property("lmname", self.__lm__)
        asr.connect('result', self.__onresult__)
                                
    def __run__(self):
        self.__pipeline__.set_state(Gst.State.PLAYING)
        self.__loop__.run()

    def __pause__(self):
        self.__pipeline__.set_state(Gst.State.PAUSED)       

    def __onresult__(self, asr, text):        
        print "result: " + text

if __name__ == '__main__':
    p = Pipeline()
    p.__run__()
'''

I feel like I prefer "answer" because "solution" can be a bit ambiguous -- does it mean a student's solution, or *the* solution? At least to me, "answer" very strongly implies "student's answer". So by using "answer" in the drop down menu for cell type, we're telling instructors to denote whether this is a cell where students will write their answers, and then additionally instructors can mark what the actual solution is by using the `### BEGIN SOLUTION` and `### END SOLUTION` syntax.

> Does this preserve the existing metadata usage so that previously created assignments will still work fine?

Mostly. There is one major change that will make things not work quite the same, which is that this now requires you to put in ids for *all* cell types rather than just grade cells. So if you want to use the same assignment after these changes take effect, you'd have to go in and make sure all the cells have ids.

This is annoying, but I really think it is a necessary change for all cells to have ids. I can't really fix things like #108 until this is the case.

> Can you still leave out the ### BEGIN/### END blocks if you select one of the answer types? Can the ### BEGIN/### END blocks be used with both manually and autograded answer types?

Yes to both questions, though I should probably add some more explicit test cases for this.

Using `deserialize_from` is problematic because it's allowed to contain multiple alternatives.

But it's a good point that using `serialized_name` as the error key is totally wrong if `deserialize_from` is set. Also, it's not possible to instantly look up the failing field based on the `serialized_name` string.

I think we should just revert to using actual field names in the error structure.

@chadrik Generating a schema, or any other usage of metadata such as `description`, is still going to require some kind of specification that describes the available properties. Since nothing of the sort exists yet, we're free to set any convention we like. This includes suggesting specific semantics for certain items within `annotations`.

If we were to separate domain-specific annotations from more universal metadata, is `description` all we'll ever need? Before storing such properties directly on the field instance, we should be reasonably sure that it won't encroach on the `__dict__` namespace too much. For example, an attribute called `"type"` could be a realistic metadata descriptor. But I would be uncomfortable reserving `BaseType.type` for that kind of purpose.

So, it's possible we should actually store the metadata properties in a container, which leads to the question of whether it's overkill to have *separate* containers for annotations and standard metadata, if the latter will *typically* only contain one or two items?

Also, on models, a `description` attribute directly on the class is not viable anyway, so it'd have to go in `_options` at minimum.
:+1: thanks for the fix.

It looks like `re` is slow to compile patterns with character classes containing explicit Unicode ranges such as `\u00A0-\uD7FF`.

Expressing "any valid character" as "any character minus everything invalid in the ASCII range" seems to not cause the same holdup, so I'll be able to fix the issue by going that route.

> If you just want to validate data you could skip the conversion step.

This is not really supported usage. Validator functions are not required to do any form of type-checking, so if the type is wrong, all bets are off — see #338. This is why `BaseType.validate()` now performs conversion automatically before running the validators.

Yeah, I kinda figured I might be breaking some coupling rules here.  But the audience for these tools is generally not computer literate, so I allowed the ease of use (which is significant) to out-weigh the loose coupling.

I've had several cases in the past where it was either essential for tools to know the input's parameter settings or the tool was greatly improved by knowing them.  But it is a bit "dirty" (not the best word to describe) to force an extra output on a tool that may not be used very often just so that a downstream tool can find its parameter settings if it needs them. Is there any chance to continue the tests execution instead of aborting.

Because the [`pytest_runtest_protocol(item, nextitem)`](https://docs.pytest.org/en/latest/writing_plugins.html#_pytest.hookspec.pytest_runtest_protocol) needs two items to execute; without at least two items, there's now way for the slave to know if that item is the last one to execute or if more might arrive in the future. IOW, if it has only one item and it executes `pytest_runtest_protocol(item, None)`, this formally means that the test session is about to end (as understood by internal and external plugins).

Things the `letsencrypt renew` will do, which re-running the client currently can't:

* Picking up extra domains that your webserver has started serving them and adding them to your cert, if your default was to ask for all domains.
* Degrading gracefully if you were asking for N domains but you no longer have one of those N domains.
* Avoid running an installer if the cert is already installed, since it isn't doing anything useful and there's a slim chance it would cause problems.

The problem you experienced is odd. In terms of modifications to Apache, the following occurred:

1. Some changes were made to your Apache configuration.
2. `/etc/init.d/apache2 reload` was executed and returned a zero status. It was assumed that the reload was successful.
3. `boulder`, the server side of Let's Encrypt, tried to perform domain validation and failed to connect to your server.
4. The changes in 1 were reverted.
5. `/etc/init.d/apache2 reload` was executed and returned a non-zero status saying Apache was not running.

This means that the reload in step 2 actually failed despite it's status of zero. Per Apache documentation, this is possible but I've never seen it in practice.

Hi Wesley,

your analysis of the producer behaviour is correct.
Since the producer is asynchronous a produce() call will just enqueue the message on an internal queue and quickly return, the actual batching and transmission to the broker is performed by a background thread which also waits for brokers acknowledgements.
What this means in practice is that if you do:
```
 p.produce(topic,msg1)
 p.poll(0)
```
Then it is highly unlikely that msg1 will have been transmitted to the broker and an ack received by the time poll() is called so it will just return immediately with nothing done (timeout=0) and the message still in transit.

`p.flush()` simply calls `p.poll(..)` until **all** `produce()`d messages have been succesfully produced to broker, or failed. That's why you dont get any message loss when you call flush() on program exit.

Regarding BufferError:
the internal producer queue is limited by `queue.buffering.max.messages` (default 100K msgs), `queue.buffering.max.kbytes` (default 400 MB).
When either of these are reached the produce() will raise BufferError and it is common to backoff for some time and then try the message send again.

There is one important thing to note here: queue.buffering.max.* accounts not only for messages being transmitted to the broker, but also those that are already produced or failed but not had their delivery report callback called (if any). For this reason an application must call `p.poll(..)` at regular intervals to serve the "reply queue" of waiting acks/errors.

There are three typical ways to do this:
 * call poll(0) after each produce. also make sure to call poll() at regular intervals if your producer loop is idle so that previously sent messages' acks are delivered.
 * call poll(..) from a separate thread that only does polling
 * call poll(..) from your program's main loop.
Until seek() is added to the Python client you can use assign() by specifying each partition's offset.

I would like to mention a [mini-project](https://github.com/abalkin/tz) that I started to implement a simple PEP 495 compliant timezone library.  Since PEP 495 implementation has not been merged yet, I wrote my code in a way that it works with Python 3.5.

As you can see below, my implementation does not suffer from this issue's bug:

```
>>> from datetime import *
>>> import tz
>>> hour = timedelta(hours=1)
>>> LA = tz.ZoneInfo.fromname('US/Pacific')
>>> t = datetime(2014, 11, 2, 1, tzinfo=LA)
>>> u = t.astimezone(timezone.utc)
>>> print(t)
2014-11-02 01:00:00-07:00
>>> print((u + hour).astimezone(LA))
2014-11-02 01:00:00-08:00
```

The disambiguation between 2014-11-02 01:00:00 PDT and 2014-11-02 01:00:00 PST is achieved by returning an instance of an auxiliary datetime subclass for the times in a fold:

```
>>> s = (u + hour).astimezone(LA)
>>> s
_DatetimeWithFold(2014, 11, 2, 1, 0, tzinfo=<tz.ZoneInfo object at 0x1046b5748>)
```
Such instances have the fold attribute which is set to 1: 
```
>>> s.fold
1
```

Starting with Python 3.6, the additional class will not be necessary and my code is written to automatically switch to using plain datetime type once it grows the fold attribute.

If you agree that this is the right approach, I can prepare a patch for distutil similar to #127 but compatible with pre-PEP 495 Python versions.

When uploading large files they are uploaded as smaller parts of 100MB and only hashes of those individual parts are calculated and used for verifying each part. The B2 server does not calculate the sha1 of the full file in those cases. You can still calculate the hash yourself and specify it using the `--sha1 `option of the `upload-file` but whatever you specifiy here is not checked by the B2 server.

There can be many rules per bucket - the command expects:

```sh
$ b2 create-bucket --lifecycleRules '[{"daysFromHidingToDeleting": 1, "daysFromUploadingToHiding": null, "fileNamePrefix": ""}]' my-very-own-test-bucket allPrivate
```

Alright I've tracked the call stack down:

```python
class ProductSchema(Schema):
    name = fields.Str()
    # implementation of nested schemas aren't relevant here
    options = fields.Nested('ProductOptionSchema', many=True) 
    variants = fields.Nested('ProdcutVariantSchema', many=True)

ps = ProductSchema()
ps.load({
  "name": "t-shirt",
  "options": [
    { "name": "size", "values": ["small", "medium", "large"] },
    { "name": "color", "values": ["red", "yellow", "blue"] },
  ],
  "variants": [
    { "sku": "red_sm", options: [ { "name": "size", "value": "small" }, { "name": "color", "value": "red" } ],
  ]
})
```

Now, let's explore the call stack, of the `load` method:

1. [`Schema.load(self, data, many=None, partial=None)`](https://github.com/marshmallow-code/marshmallow/blob/327530deee83073eb8d5f0fc80df3601e4320bd4/marshmallow/schema.py#L479) calls ->
2. [`Schema._do_load(self, data, many=None, partial=None, postprocess=True)`](https://github.com/marshmallow-code/marshmallow/blob/327530deee83073eb8d5f0fc80df3601e4320bd4/marshmallow/schema.py#L549) calls ->
3. [`Unmarshaller.deserialize(self, data, fields_dict, many=False, partial=False, dict_class=dict, index_errors=True, index=None)`](https://github.com/marshmallow-code/marshmallow/blob/327530deee83073eb8d5f0fc80df3601e4320bd4/marshmallow/marshalling.py#L214) executes ->
```python
        for attr_name, field_obj in iteritems(fields_dict):
            if field_obj.dump_only:
                continue
            field_name = attr_name
            if field_obj.load_from:
                field_name = field_obj.load_from
            try:
                raw_value = data.get(field_name, missing)
            # ... continues
```

With the answer being that we're iterating over `fields_dict` (the schema instance's `fields` attribute) which gets set to an `OrderedDict` (if `Schema.Meta.ordered = True`) in [`_update_fields(self, obj=None, many=False)`](https://github.com/marshmallow-code/marshmallow/blob/327530deee83073eb8d5f0fc80df3601e4320bd4/marshmallow/schema.py#L658) ... called in `Schema.__init__`.

So really, all I'm looking for is confirmation that this implementation is as expected, and not subject to change. Probably, would require an update to the [documentation for `Meta.ordered`](http://marshmallow.readthedocs.io/en/latest/api_reference.html#marshmallow.Schema.Meta) which is currently:
```
* ordered: If True, order serialization output according to the order in which fields were declared. Output of Schema.dump will be a collections.OrderedDict.
```

and could possibly be updated by revising to:

```
* ordered: If True, order serialization output according to the order in which fields were declared. Output of Schema.dump will be a collections.OrderedDict. Also, deserialization will occur according to the order in which fields were declared, enabling a deterministic loading strategy.
```

> If dump_only meant "don't allow loading", then unknown=ERROR | INCLUDE would error and only explicitly opting into unknown=EXCLUDE would allow the data to be discarded.

Wouldn't it be be confusing if `unknown=INCLUDE` actually raised error...?

There's also this way to look at things:

1) Regard `dump_only` fields as _unknown_ (`INCLUDE` would include them, `RAISE` would raise them). This is the current behavior.
2) Regard `dump_only` fields as _known and acknowledged_ (`INCLUDE` would ignore them, `RAISE` would ignore them). This is what this ticket suggests.

I think in that sense the current behavior (1) is _consistent_ between `INCLUDE`/`RAISE`. New behavior (2) could be enabled by different `unknown` choice (e.g. `INCLUDE_XXX`, `RAISE_XXX`). But maybe it makes sense to consider the use cases first. Aligned with the above numbering:

1) As already mentioned, "read-only" fields, e.g. "created_at". But when would you want to use `INCLUDE` rather than `RAISE` here? Why allow client send fields that are not specified?
2) I don't actually know - in which cases would we want to ignore these fields during load-time?

So is there actual need for (2)? I'm actually starting to think current behavior is correct. Maybe @lafrech and @deckar01 could clarify their use cases?

> we would either need to soften the "unknown" validation -- harden the definition of dump_only so that it doesn't violate the expectations set by the unknown option.

- The `ValidationError` seems to only support a single error, but supports associating the error with multiple fields.
 - The `@validate_scheme` decorator can only handle a single exception that gets raised.
 - The `fields` parameter probably accepts fields regardless of how deeply they are nested, but `field_names` has no way to express nested field names.

A common design pattern is for a validator to return a list of errors. Nested field names are starting to be expressed using [dotted nesting syntax](https://github.com/marshmallow-code/marshmallow/pull/405#issuecomment-220862224).

```py
@marshmallow.validates_schema
def validate_all(self, data):
    errors = []
    if data['foo']['bar'] < data['baz']['bam']:
        errors.append(ValidationError('Should be less than baz.bam', ['foo.bar']))
    if data['foo']['quux'] < data['baz']['bam']:
        errors.append(ValidationError('Should be less than baz.bam', ['foo.quux']))
    ...
    return errors
```

I don't think `only` is allowed either.

The common way to do that is to share the Schema class but not the instance, and instantiate the schema with different options when needed. See discussion here: https://github.com/marshmallow-code/marshmallow/issues/761.

The situation that @fluffels has described is one in which you have a Resource A with M2M related Resource B. For Resource A you want to be able to change the instances of Resource B associated with an instance of Resource A whether it's a POST or a PATCH that creates/changes that link. 

What you don't want is for someone to be able to POST a Resource A instance that includes an inline creation for an instance of Resource B.

So, you configure Resource B to be read-only and you configure the relation between Resource A and Resource B to be modifiable.

This worked fine in the 0.12.x line and the 0.13.x line has introduced the buggy behaviour described by @fluffels. Your advice that he set the M2M field to readonly completely misses the point of the desired behaviour.

I imagine this is a side effect of the peculiar way that Tastypie seems to perform PATCHes.

The AOI and temperature coefficients are keyword arguments in the function definitions in pvsystem.py. The pvsystem.PVSystem class methods respect the function defaults if your PVSystem object does not define them in its module_parameters attribute.

I don't think the problem is actually in pvsystem, though, because your error message sounds like a ModelChain initialization error. ModelChain tries to infer which models to use based on the coefficient names in the PVSystem's module_parameters attribute. However, the SAM module definitions do not include these parameters, so you must either add them to the module_parameters attribute or explicitly set some of the ModelChain methods.

This sounds like a cool idea, however having looked at more closely I think it poses a few challenges:

- there doesn't seem to be good cross-browser support for dynamically changing and refreshing the favicon without also forcing a page reload (at least not that I could find). Even if we managed to find a workaround for this, we'll then have to generate and distribute static .ico files of our own if we want to display the same font-awesome icons as used elsewhere in MMW. This adds a bit of a maintenance burden going forward (as we'll have to keep this in sync with each font-awesome release) for a fairly minor UI tweak.
- If resorting to plain text and unicode characters, then there also [aren't any consistent unicode characters for 'pause'](http://www.unicode.org/mail-arch/unicode-ml/y2005-m08/0367.html) to use along with [▶ - 'black right pointing arrow'](http://www.fileformat.info/info/unicode/char/25B6/index.htm). All of the alternatives that I tried looked weird.
- (probably most importantly) changing the title to include details of the currently playing song pollutes the browser history: the browser history will show ``[play status symbol] [now playing] - [current menuitem] | Mopidy on [hostname]``, while the URL for that history entry only contains the hash to navigate to ``[current menu item]``. It might be surprising to users if they click on a history entry like this expecting to hear the same song again while a different one is currently playing (or worse still, paused).

Having said all of that, I think it would be useful to at least include ``[current menu item]``, as this will make it easier to select a specific menu items again when going back in the browser history.
Closing this then, in favor of proposing the changes to the Mopidy core.

Thank you for the report! This is a bit unfortunate indeed. One could argue that transposed convolution is the backward pass of a vanilla convolution, and the vanilla convolution will get its weights initialized correctly, but often you'll use the transposed convolution as a shortcut of dilating the input and applying a vanilla convolution.
I'm not quite sure how to address this. Transposing the weights is not really an option, it was done this way on purpose so the weights have the memory layout expected by the backward pass convolution implementations. We could add an option to the `HeUniform` constructor signalling that weights are transposed, or a general `lasagne.init.transposed` wrapper for all initializers. Note that the current default initializer, `GlorotUniform`, is not affected since it balances the forward and backward pass requirements. So we could also opt to not care, or to note this in the documentation of the TransposedConv2DLayer.

What's your use case? Do you specifically need to use He-style initialization?

I like this idea.  I can't think of a way to allow this and still allow pre-computing the input, unless we put the input-to-hidden connections outside of the recurrence, but that's ok because we'd have to sacrifice this for custom LSTM or GRU layers anyways.  But, unless I'm missing something, that's another really good reason to keep the current layers too.

The only open question is (@benanne, @ebenolson): Do we want to warn about the "deterministic" keyword for a network without dropouts/noise or do we want to allow "deterministic" no matter what? This could be implemented by changing [a single line](https://github.com/Lasagne/Lasagne/pull/585/files#diff-84ce884e5f993565a6a1db7b062daa20R154), so it's just a question of what we'd prefer.

An argument for always allowing "deterministic" is that it would encourage people to always pass `deterministic=True` when creating the validation/test graph, making it less likely to be forgotten. If there's a warning as long as you don't use dropout layers, people may remove it (possibly without understanding) and not add it back later. What do you think?

@umayaml allowing users to define I/O parameters (e.g., chunking, compression etc.) for datasets is useful. This will have to be on a per-dataset basis.  Enabling compression globally is not a good idea. Compression in HDF5 depends also on enabling chunking on data. Both compression and chunking can have severe impact on read/write speeds and enabling them blindly for everything is going to lead to problems very quickly.

There isn't one, really. VEX is SSA for the temporary storage variables within a basic block. Other than that, registers and memory aren't SSA per-se. You can always uniquely identify a memory or register write by a tuple of (basic_block_addr, statement_id), but the merging through phi nodes and so forth, if you need an analysis like that, is not currently done.

vex is indeed poor for x87 support. This isn't really a limitation of the IR itself, just valgrind's lifter is missing support for it. I think what happens is that it treats all 80 bit floats as just doubles, and then has a special dirty op to convert to/from the 80 bit format when it's stored in memory.

Another issue you will run into is that z3 doesn't have support for floating point theory of the long double (80bit) floats.

@squilter @a7scenario As you [can see here](https://github.com/squilter/dronekit-python/blob/versions_and_capability/dronekit/__init__.py#L2011), the rate is set only during initialisation. That means that if the rate is changed later by the GCS on the same channel, then DroneKit is doing nothing about that.

If you're seeing this stick then either the the GCS and dronekit are on a different mavlink channels or the GCS you're using is not trying to set the rate (some continually reset it, and others do not).

I do not particularly have a problem with us having an API to set rate, but it really should be considered a "desired rate" given that other systems on the channel can change it. 

What I would recommend is that if your app does depend on a certain rate, observing the [SRX_](http://copter.ardupilot.com/wiki/arducopter-parameters/#sr0__parameters) parameters to monitor that the rate is OK. At least then you have more debug on the problem configurations.

我刚才也遇到了一样的问题。

不仅仅是--plot选项, 所有在扩展mod中增加的选项如--plot-save都无效.

根源在在__init__.py文件中的这个语句:
from rqalpha.__main__ import cli
这导致__main__.py文件被执行两次, 生成了两个cli变量, 一个在__init__.py中, 一个在__main__.py中, inject_mod_commands修改的是__init__.py中的cli, 而__main__.py运行时使用的是__main__.py中的cli.

有几个解决办法, 改动最小的解决办法是在__main__.py的entry_point函数中增加一句'from rqalpha import cli', 如下:

```
def entry_point():
    inject_mod_commands()
    from rqalpha import cli
    cli(obj={})
```

但这种方法这只是掩盖了代码上的问题,  要彻底解决问题还是需要重新组织__main__.py和__init__.py中的代码, 在入口语句前不要交叉引用。

Why does there have to be some distinction between _local_ and _remote_ file objects?
I think we can and should avoid this.
This is something we can accommodate more or less with the existing schema, the only distinction is that the `localDir` attribute should be considered the source/target variable for `get/put` and not the absolute location of the file on a locally accessible disk.

The point of this is that I may decide when writing a RTHandler for instance that I want a file to be placed somewhere for another task (for instance in GaudiExec where I want all files except DiracFile to be placed in a directory so I can upload 1 large tarball to DIRAC before job submit).
In this case I expect to be able to do something sensible for every file type and not have to write a list of `if else` testing the file type which just feels unpythonic and as if we're dropping the point of having a nicely defined interface in the first place.

I want a command or set of common commands which satisfy the 'make me a copy of that file here'. I don't want to have to worry about where the file is coming from or how it's getting there I just want a copy there and the IGangaFile class should contain enough ability/information to do this.

A more sensible solution is to introduce a `location` schema attribute in `LocalFile` which will default to be the same as `localDir` when it's not defined for backward compatibility. This means that existing jobs and functionality load and work correctly whilst allowing future jobs to work as expected.
(The introduction of this breaks forwards compatibility but this is the price we pay as with the `defaultSE` on the `DiracFile`).
From there we can define the `location` to be the location of the disk as the user defined the file (upon construction) and we can re-define (internally for our understanding) the `localDir` to be what we want to be able to use the `get` and `put` methods appropriately.
I was wanting to avoid this particular way of working as it changes the schema and whenever this changes there are always headaches. Especially when it comes to the `LocalFile` which is used by pretty much every analysis using the `inputfiles`.


As an aside:
-----aside-----
I __really__ disagree with putting the logic for defining the output directory of files in the `get`/`put` methods as this should probably be done in some qualifying step which is common to all files which gives all files the same ability as the naming schemes in `MassStorageFile`.
-----aside-----

We just talked about this in Vidyo -- for those following along at home:

- Although this issue seemed like a P5 previously, maybe it's a P3 or P2. @leplatrem , @mostlygeek ?
- I agree that the existing query is bad and needs to be replaced. I'm not excited about replacing it with two queries: this introduces race conditions, and for some requests, it's slower than what we have.
- I think we can do better than what we have without introducing race conditions nor slowing down certain queries. I think that would be a slam dunk easy merge scenario.
- If we're going to make some requests slower in exchange for making other requests faster, I think we need to have very clear and explicit reasoning about why the requests we're making slower are OK to make slower (less common, less important, etc.).
- An option might be to introduce heuristics to try to figure out which kind of situation we're in -- maybe in situation A, query q1 is faster, and in situation B, query q2 is faster. (We do have some stuff like this already; see https://github.com/Kinto/kinto/blob/382adcdc2dccff7151798bc7d250769dc818d83e/kinto/core/permission/postgresql/__init__.py#L482-L493.) But this gets us into the business of trying to outrun the Postgres query planner and I'm not confident that our time is well spent doing that.

I spent some time looking into this today and I noticed a few things. I changed `wait_for_frames()` to return an empty frameset instead of throwing an error and saw the following:

1. If `wait_for_frames(40)` times out, it does not necessarily mean a frame was dropped. When I examine the deltas between frames, occasionally I see instances where where an empty frameset is returned, but the deltas for the few frames before & after are all around 33ms, so it doesn't look like anything was dropped
2. If a frame actually was dropped, it seems to be reliably picked up in the delta calculation when the next non-empty frameset arrives, i.e. on the times that there are empty framesets where frames were dropped, in the next frameset or 2 I'll see a delta of ~60-100ms
3. Calculating frame deltas can't reliably calculate how many frames were dropped. For example, if I calculate a delta of 85ms between two frames on a 30fps stream, does that mean 2 frames were dropped, or 3?

So it seems that trying to detect dropped frames via a timeout on `wait_for_frames()` is redundant since the deltas should pick it up, and unreliable as a counter since the deltas are not necessarily close to the multiples of 33 (i.e. 66, 99, 132, etc.). In the log below they are, though I have also seen instances where the deltas have been 84 (approx 33ms x 2.5), 152 (approx 33 x 4.6), 116 (33 x 3.5).

The specific issue is that during installation the installer is not finding a GLFW3 library.  This is pretty normal, as most folks probably don't have GLFW3 installed separately (mujoco comes with its own copy of the library).

So we have to tell the installer where it is, but this is surprisingly hard to do from setuputils/distutils (modify the path used to load/link libraries).  Instead we just set the environment variable that the loader/linker sees and deal with it that way.

This is what the environment variable at the start of the line is doing -- only setting the environment variable for that one command.
We're not exporting (saving) the new value, so we don't have to append to the path.  This variable shouldn't be needed for anything other than installation.

After the python package is installed, we're able to fixup the load/link path at runtime, so that `import mujoco_py` successfully finds both the GLFW3 and libmujoco libraries.

Me gustaría que la filosofía de los métodos de DataJson sea la siguiente:
Los argumentos a los métodos se llamarán *según los elementos que manipulan*, y no según los *nombres de sus representaciones*, aunque efectivamente reciban una representación del elemento.
 Así, por ejemplo, validate_catalog() tendrá un argumento `catalog` (en lugar de `datajson_path_or_url`), que acepta una *representación* aceptable de un catálogo (un diccionario, o una str con un path a un archivo en formato JSON que describe un diccionario). 
Todo método de DataJson() que acepte un parámetro `catalog(s)`, deberá, como primer acción, transformar la representación externa del catálogo a una representación interna, en formato de diccionario.

Similarmente, si un método recibe un argumento `report`, el input deberá ser una representación externa de un reporte (es decir, un path a un CSV/XLSX o una lista de diccionarios), y lo primero que hace es transformar ese input a la represetación interna de un reporte (lista de diccionarios).

Hi @kootenpv,

The vocabulary corresponding to a text file can easily be built using other tools as a preprocessing step to `fasttext print-vectors`, which is the reason why it uses streaming. For example, you can use the command line:
```bash
cat <text-file> | tr -s "[:space:]" "\n" | sort | uniq
```
or the (more efficient) python script:
```python
import sys

vocab = {}
for line in sys.stdin:
    for word in line.split():
        vocab[word] = True

for k in vocab:
    print k
```
Hope this answers your question. Best.

For all of the char types, it decodes to the UTF-*n* encoding in native byte order, then returns the first `char`/`char16_t`/etc. element.  (For UTF-16 and -32, the BOM that Python always adds to the encoded string is skipped).

For `char` you're right that this is going to be wrong (not only for U+100 and above, but also for U+80 and above): the first byte is going to be just the `char` value of the initial byte of a multibyte UTF-8 sequence.  We should probably fix it to return the unicode codepoint for 0-255, and throw for anything above 255.  (Though `char` is signed, so the 128-255 need to become the appropriate negative, i.e. via a `reinterpret_cast<char>((unsigned char) value)`).  Moreover I think we should do this *before* we tell Python to encode as UTF-8.

For `char16_t` (and `wchar_t` on Windows) we should probably be throwing the same sort of error for >U+FFFF values.  Endian issues aren't a concern because we're never converting to bytes, but just keeping everything in native 16-bit integers.  (Or to put it another way, endianness is a concern for the `std::u16string` user, above the pybind11 level).

Now that I think about it, I'm not sure why we aren't also checking the `src` string length: I'd *expect* an exception if I tried to call a function taking a `char` with a string, but the current behaviour is to just take the first `char` value.

I think freezing the `IDisplay`s isn't right... You commented earlier that it's okay if the return_value on `IDisplay` objects is set by the user. If the `IDisplay`s and the mock `getUtility` are both `FreezableMock`, freezing must disallow changing the `return_value` of a `FreezableMock` to address the original intent of the PR, which was to prevent developers from reassigning the `getUtility` return_value to vanilla Mock objects, which would nullify any `_func`. Freezing the `getUtility` mock accomplishes the original intent. Meanwhile, the `IDisplay` objects must allow changing their `return_value` for meaningful tests*, so they must not be frozen at initialization.

Here's roughly what I'm doing to check the criteria:
```
import zope
from certbot.tests.util import patch_get_utility
from certbot import interfaces
import pdb
with patch_get_utility():
    get = zope.component.getUtility #Frozen Freezable
    foo = zope.component.getUtility("foo") #Frozen Freezable

    try: # Expect key error raised by _func
        print "foo.yesno(): %s" %(foo.yesno())
    except KeyError:
        print "Got expected KeyError invoking foo.yesno()"
    print "Correctly invoked foo.yesno('okay', default=True): %s" %(foo.yesno('okay', default=True))

    try: # Expect this to fail. This is the original case outlined in issue. get represents the mock getUtility
        get.return_value = 1
    except AttributeError:
        print "Got expected attributeError during: %s" %("get.return_value = 1")
    else:
        raise Exception("Succeeded setting return value on %s" %("get.return_value = 1"))

    try: # Expect this to fail too. foo is the display returned by getUtility which presents IDisplay objects to developer
        foo.return_value = 1
    except AttributeError:
        print "Got expected attributeError during: %s" %("foo.return_value = 1")
    else:
        raise Exception("Succeeded setting return value on %s" %("foo.return_value = 1"))

    try: # But changing the return_value on an IDisplay mock should work.
        foo.yesno.return_value = 1
    except AttributeError:
        raise Exception("Should not get an attributeError during: %s" %("foo.yesno.return_value = 1"))
    else:
        print "Succeeded setting return value on %s" %("foo.yesno.return_value = 1")
```

Thanks @Anonymouslemming. With the additional information I was able to reproduce this as well. The problem in your case is caused by having two versions of Certbot/letsencrypt installed. You have one from `pip` and one from `apt`. When `certbot` runs, these files are being mixed and causing conflicts and the error message you see here.

I was able to fix the problem by running `apt purge python-letsencrypt`. Alternatively, you can use [certbot-auto](https://certbot.eff.org/docs/install.html#certbot-auto) which creates an isolated Certbot install that won't be affected by your mixed installation.

Based on your comment, I believe you already saw it but for anyone else who comes across this, see our warning about getting yourself into this situation [here](https://certbot.eff.org/docs/install.html#installing-from-source). Since this caused by people not following installation instructions, corrupting files on their system, and this problem isn't fatal (just annoying), I don't think it's worth us investing engineering effort to work around these broken installations. I'm marking this as "wontfix".

Yes. The project has a long history of requiring `sudo`. `certbot` is in many ways a system tool. It often needs to bind to 80/443, modify web server configuration files, restart your web server, etc. This leads to annoyances like the one found here, but it was the decision made in the design of the project.

I don't think falling back to alternative directories is a good idea. What happens when you forget and accidentally run the the client without `sudo`? Doing this would complicate our code, documentation, and the experience for the user so I think this isn't a path we should go down.

At the same time, the error message you got is pretty awful and #2734 aims to improve it. In the mean time, you can specify alternative directories using the `--config-dir`, `--work-dir`, and `--logs-dir` flags. You can specify this in a [configuration file](https://certbot.eff.org/docs/using.html#configuration-file) which makes this slightly less cumbersome.
