Doing this allows us to remove Python 2.6 support which fixes or helps make progress on a lot of issues like #3726, #4244, #4377, and #4379. @SwartzCr put me in touch with some people from Red Hat who told us how to reliably and safely do this programmaticly. Here's what I learned:

OK, so the first thing to do would be to get a copy of Python 2.7 onto the system. RHEL and CentOS 6 both provide a technology called "Software Collections" that allow users to install a version of Python 2.7 (along with many other useful tools) outside of the usual system paths.

On CentOS, this is accomplished by installing the `centos-release-scl` package (`yum install centos-release-scl`) followed by `yum install python27`.

On RHEL, this may depend on which subscriptions are available; it may be necessary to add the "Optional" set of repositories to the RHEL subscription in order to install the python27 package.
```
subscription-manager repos --enable rhel-6-server-optional-rpms
subscription-manager repos --enable rhel-server-rhscl-6-rpms
```
On some systems, `rhn-channel` must be used instead of subscription manager. You can determine which program must be used by running:
```
yum -C repolist |grep 'receiving updates from RHN Classic'
```

or
```
yum -C repolist |grep 'receiving updates from Red Hat Subscription Management'
```
More info about how to do this can be found at: https://access.redhat.com/solutions/472163

After that, `yum install python27` should work.

Once those packages are installed, it will be possible to issue commands to this python27 installation by using the following syntax: scl enable python27 'command --arg1 --arg2'

Alternately, you can have your tools wrapped in a bash script that will `source /opt/rh/python27/enable` which will set your environment variables appropriately so that paths and other things are looking in the right places. This file is just environment variables, so you can probably parse it and set it in your script directly as well.

Additional resources:

* https://www.madboa.com/blog/2016/08/29/scl-intro/
* https://access.redhat.com/documentation/en-US/Red_Hat_Software_Collections/2-Beta/html/Packaging_Guide/sect-Enabling_the_Software_Collection.html

The main task behind this issue is to parallelize execution of some binaries used for calculation of particle passage through matter (Monte-Carlo methods).

These binaries take some input files (one or several text files) and produce several output files. The idea of parallelization is to:
 - create several temp directories, copy (or link) there input files
 - run the code in parallel on separate processes (most effectively number of processes limited by number of CPU cores)
 - get the output from these temp directories, merge it and save somewhere

The code should also nicely handle situation when binaries are crashing or producing no output.

Few steps to get started with this issue, to get acquainted with the problem:

- [ ] get the DEMO version of SHIELD-HIT12A program here: http://shieldhit.org/index.php?id=download  (it can simulate up to 10000 particles)
- [ ] get some glimpse of documentation included here: http://neptun.phys.au.dk/~bassler/SHIELD_HIT/SHIELDHIT12A_UsersGuide.pdf
- [ ] run some examples (best on Linux) included in the DEMO package
- [ ] try to run pymchelper/utils/runmc.py from this PR

It would be worth to try also infrastructure available at PLGRID (Cyfronet) where machines with 12 cores are available.

ok, I can ensure order so `sos_step('a')` would essentially be `sos_workflow('a')`, which actually make the latter unnecessary because `sos_workflow('a+b')` is rarely needed and users can use the extra-step trick if needed.

The problem with the implementation is that we are using separate DAGs for `sos_run('a')` but in this case the steps would be added to the master DAG, which assumes one numerically ordered forward-style trunk. Adding multiple numerically ordered mini-workflows would complicate the DAG building (but not necessarily difficult though).

The current implementation needs to also check for old GPUs. Currently if a system has CUDA and cuDNN installed but has a GPU with compute capability <3, it results in failure, while all the checks are seemingly fine! Incorporating a simple check like the one below should fix this issue : 
```
def is_gpu_supported():
    for d in range(torch.cuda.device_count()):
        capability = torch.cuda.get_device_capability(d)[0]
        return False if capability < 3 else True

USE_GPU = torch.cuda.is_available()
def to_gpu(x, *args, **kwargs):
    '''puts pytorch variable to gpu, if cuda is available and USE_GPU is set to true. '''
    return x.cuda(*args, **kwargs) if (USE_GPU and is_gpu_supported()) else x
```

I'd say that it's fairly easy to write a new loader, and also more generic, because this format conversion is specific to PIL.

We can improve the documentation of the `loader` to make it explicit that it users PIL and converts the image to 'RGB' format, but I don't think it's necessary to pass yet another argument to the constructor of the `ImageFolder`.

For reference, here is how I'd write a loader to always convert to `RGBA` format:
```python
def pil_loader_rgba(path):
    # open path as file to avoid ResourceWarning (https://github.com/python-pillow/Pillow/issues/835)
    with open(path, 'rb') as f:
        img = Image.open(f)
        return img.convert('RGBA')
```

How would this change if there would be a playlist implementation? Streamlink would still need to request the API/website to retrieve the stream data, which ofc has a slight delay. By using the hls passthrough, Streamlink doesn't know about the current position in the currently playing stream, because the player is fetching the data instead of Streamlink, so it can't do these requests ahead of time.

This is the **normal** and the **expected behavior**,
see https://github.com/requests/requests/issues/3899

There is also a workaround  for this,
put the the query directly in the URL.

https://github.com/requests/requests/issues/3899#issuecomment-283223303

```
$ streamlink http://127.0.0.1 --http-query-param "foo=**"
error: Unable to open URL: http://127.0.0.1 (HTTPConnectionPool(host='127.0.0.1', port=80):
Max retries exceeded with url: /?foo=%2A%2A
```
```
$ streamlink http://127.0.0.1?foo=**
error: Unable to open URL: http://127.0.0.1?foo=** (HTTPConnectionPool(host='127.0.0.1', port=80):
```

Hi, just to give some feedback, from my perspective this simply broke backwards compatibility with the previous, widespread version (and this bugreport is a bit hard to find and I didn't find a note about this in the changes list). Perhaps it was intended as a bugfix for a very old bug, but I wish it was handled a bit more carefully, perhaps with a deprecation warning and a suggestion of how to do this the new way. I would have never thought https://xkcd.com/1172/ would apply to me, but apparently... :)

Another thing is how to do things "properly", which was terribly unclear to me. I'll describe the solution we reached for benefit of other visitors of this bug; perhaps there's a better or more proper way, but the documentation and examples are short on anything non-trivial. We use `interactive()` so that we can have a custom widget layout (we can explore a set that has previous/next buttons aside of a slider), but call `display()` from inside the function and never explicitly render the widget returned by `interactive()`, which we thought is how we're supposed to do it. This stopped working with the upgrade from 5.0. Furthermore, to get the first item in the set rendered before any user interaction, we used an `.on_display()` hook on the IntSlider to call the function for the first time. (c.f. e.g. http://stackoverflow.com/questions/29332777/widget-layout-when-using-interact#comment53554728_29332968)

What we do now is that we `display()` our custom layout together with `interactive().children[-1]`, which is the function output widget and which gets refreshed properly. The on_display trick stops working though, because if the function is called outside of `interactive()` context, it doesn't render into the output widget and therefore we get multiple renders in the cell when we start interacting. But it seems there's another trick now, calling `interactive.update()`.  This is the code we are using now:

```
    from ipywidgets import IntSlider, Button, VBox, HBox, interactive

    def show_widget(i):
        ...

    i_slider = IntSlider(description='Item:', value=start, min=0, max=len(dssplit) - 1, step=1, continuous_update=False)

    def show_prev(_):
        i_slider.value -= 1
    prev_button = Button(description='Previous')
    prev_button.on_click(show_prev)

    def show_next(_):
        i_slider.value += 1
    next_button = Button(description='Next')
    next_button.on_click(show_next)

    intwidget = interactive(show_widget, i=i_slider)
    intoutput = intwidget.children[-1]
    display(VBox([i_slider, HBox([prev_button, next_button]), intoutput]))
    intwidget.update()
```

The main problem I see with a hybrid `class TrasyncioCancelled` is that it's now an Exception, not just a BaseException, so any `except Exception:` in trio code will catch it. Not good. I propose to monkey-patch `asnycio.CancelledError` instead: simply replace it with `trio.Cancelled`.

Another approach might be to replace asyncio.* entirely. I.e. `sys.modules['asyncio']=importlb.import_module("trio.asyncio")`. Ditto its submodules, if necessary. I wonder if that might not actually be less work overall.

```python
from functools import partial as P
```

The appveyor ci builds also use `-s` to improve our chances of debugging weird and intermittent issues. So there's potentially some benefit to reducing unnecessary spamminess anyway.

Those messages are generated by the test suite only (or should be, anyway!). I'm pretty sure they come from this wacky custom transport stream that I wrote to test renegotiation. Now that it's working we could potentially dial down the spamminess though.

In general there is some question of whether and how trio should log things. For the most part trio is low level enough that this doesn't come up, but there are a few places where it might be useful (logging errors in `Instrument`s, or when we get a high-level `accept` loop wrapper, logging connections dropped due to lack of resources). The problem is that the stdlib logging system is not that great, so we don't necessarily want to force our users into it. But I also don't want to create our own custom logging system, like e.g. twisted does... Probably they should be discussed on a different issue though :-)

I made my own attempt at implementing this functionality as generic as possible and ended up with the following snippet, which is somewhat in between the `dogpile.cache Lock` and the `SharedTask` in your PR:
```python
@attr.s()
class CacheValue(object):
    value = attr.ib()  # type: Result
    creator = attr.ib()  # type: Callable
    create_lock = attr.ib()  # type: Lock

    def is_set(self):
        return self.value is not None

    def is_creating(self):
        return self.create_lock.locked()

    async def get_or_create(self):
        if not self.is_set():
            async with self.create_lock:
                if not self.is_set():
                    self.value = await Result.acapture(self.creator())

        return self.value.unwrap()

    async def reset(self):
        async with self.create_lock:
            self.value = None
```
Basically, you need some variant of the following functionality:
- some kind of per-value synchronization primitives (i.e. `Event` or `Lock`, possibly created lazily using a global `Lock`, but possibly also inter-process synchronization)
- a way of capturing / caching a single outcome (e.g. `Result.acapture`), with various customizations:
  - immediately discarding certain results, e.g, (certain) exceptions, invalid values, etc.
  - storing the result externally (e.g. DBM file, redis, memcached,...)
  - expiring values immediately after creation (so that only concurrent calls will be coalesced) or after some timeout after its creation or last access (resulting in a time or size limited cache)
  - retrying on invalid results
  - further cache-related features... (compare e.g. the [Guava Cache for Java](https://github.com/google/guava/wiki/CachesExplained))
- cancellation handling (propagate the exception to all waiters / only the first one and let the second one retry / ...)
- a way of mapping function calls (i.e. function + `self` + `*args` + `**kwargs`) to cache keys

With all the different use-cases and requirements that may arise I'm unsure whether a really generic implementation in the trio library would be that easy to make. Maybe some examples for basic functionality (like my snippet above, with some more thoughts and explanations put into cancellation) plus libraries for more targeted use-cases (like aiocache, which actually provides a synchronized [`cached_stampede`](https://github.com/argaen/aiocache/blob/master/aiocache/decorators.py#L113) decorator -- which still seems to have [cancellation problems](https://github.com/argaen/aiocache/issues/393) though) would be a better approach.

For my use-case, I'll probably go with some combination of [pyfailsafe](https://github.com/Skyscanner/pyfailsafe) and the aiocache `cached_stampede`, using the in-memory backend.

The one in trio's test suite is harmless; it's a mild bug in trio's version of `@acontextmanager`, that's fixed in `async_generator.asynccontextmanager`. So the fix there is https://github.com/python-trio/trio/issues/418 :-)

The broader problem here is that there's no really decent way to handle garbage collection for async generators. For normal generators, if they're garbage collected, then the interpreter throws in an exception to run any finally blocks. (That's part of [PEP 342](https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0342/); before this, it simply wasn't allowed to `yield` inside a `try` or `with` block.) For async generators, the interpreter wants to do the same thing... But if you want to throw an exception into an async generator, you need to call `athrow`, which is async, so you can't call it from there garbage collector. Only the coroutine runner can actually do this.

[PEP 525](https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0525/) tried to work around this by adding some hooks that a coroutine runner can register to get notified when the GC wants to kill an async generator. It's kind of messy, and in particular is problematic for trio, since there's no way to make the cleanup happen in the right task context (https://github.com/python-trio/trio/issues/265). This is particularly bad because there are some common `with` blocks that *must* be cleaned up in the right context, ie nurseries – though the problems with generators and trio's nurseries and cancel scopes to beyond just cleanup (https://github.com/python-trio/trio/issues/264).

[PEP 533](https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0533/) is an alternative proposal I wrote, but it hasn't managed to get any momentum so far.

There are also wackier options. For example, Curio analyses the byte code of async generators to figure out whether they actually have any of the tricky constructs like a `yield` inside a `try`, and tries to only apply special handling to the tricky generators, while leaving simple cases like your example alone.

Anyway, it's a mess. So far async_generator has tried to stay out of this mess by using the simplest possible rule: tell users they should close their async generators explicitly (e.g. using the `aclosing` context manager we helpfully provide), and issue a warning if they don't.

It's possible we should grit our teeth and implement the gc dance specified in PEP 525 (at least for now), to match native async generators. (Fun fact: curio's bytecode inspection will give the wrong results if we do this, because it runs via the PEP 525 hooks and can't recognize that `await yield_()` is a `yield`. I'm less worried about curio compatibility than I once was though...)
